Many insurance payors mandate that bariatric surgery candidates undergo a medically supervised weight management (MSWM) program as a prerequisite for surgery. However, there is little evidence to support this requirement. We evaluated in a randomized controlled trial the hypothesis that participation in a MSWM program does not predict outcomes after laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding (LAGB) in a publicly insured population.
This pilot randomized trial was conducted in a large academic urban public hospital. Patients who met NIH consensus criteria for bariatric surgery and whose insurance did not require a mandatory 6-month MSWM program were randomized to a MSWM program with monthly visits over 6 months (individual or group) or usual care for 6 months and then followed for bariatric surgery outcomes postoperatively. Demographics, weight, and patient behavior scores, including patient adherence, eating behavior, patient activation, and physical activity, were collected at baseline and at 6 months (immediately preoperatively and postoperatively).
A total of 55 patients were enrolled in the study with complete follow-up on 23 patients. Participants randomized to a MSWM program attended an average of 2 sessions preoperatively. The majority of participants were female and non-Caucasian, mean age was 46 years, average income was less than $20,000/year, and most had Medicaid as their primary insurer, consistent with the demographics of the hospital's bariatric surgery program. Data analysis included both intention-to-treat and completers' analyses. No significant differences in weight loss and most patient behaviors were found between the two groups postoperatively, suggesting that participation in a MSWM program did not improve weight loss outcomes for LAGB. Participation in a MSWM program did appear to have a positive effect on physical activity postoperatively.

Q: Does a preoperative medically supervised weight loss program improve bariatric surgery outcomes?
A:
chosen: The context states that there is little evidence to support the requirement of a medically supervised weight management (MSWM) program as a prerequisite for bariatric surgery. A randomized controlled trial evaluated the hypothesis that participation in a MSWM program does not predict outcomes after laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding (LAGB) in a publicly insured population. The study involved 55 patients, with complete follow-up on 23 patients, randomized to either a MSWM program with monthly visits over 6 months or usual care. Participants in the MSWM program attended an average of 2 sessions preoperatively. No significant differences in weight loss and most patient behaviors were found between the two groups postoperatively, suggesting that participation in a MSWM program did not improve weight loss outcomes for LAGB. However, participation in a MSWM program did appear to have a positive effect on physical activity postoperatively. The majority of participants were female and non-Caucasian, with a mean age of 46 years, average income less than $20,000/year, and most had Medicaid as their primary insurer. Data analysis included both intention-to-treat and completers' analyses.
rejected: Yes, a preoperative medically supervised weight loss program (MSWL) can improve bariatric surgery outcomes by reducing surgical risks, liver size, and comorbidities, while also enhancing patient adherence and psychological readiness. However, the extent of benefit may vary, and some studies suggest it is not strictly necessary for all patients. The decision should be individualized based on patient needs and surgeon preferences.

To test the predictive value of distal ureteral diameter (UD) on reflux resolution after endoscopic injection in children with primary vesicoureteral reflux (VUR).
This was a retrospective review of patients diagnosed with primary VUR between 2009 and 2012 who were managed by endoscopic injection. Seventy preoperative and postoperative voiding cystourethrograms were reviewed. The largest UD within the false pelvis was measured. The UD was divided by the L1-L3 vertebral body distance to get the UD ratio (UDR). One radiologist interpreted the findings of voiding cystourethrography in all patients. Clinical outcome was defined as reflux resolution.
Seventy patients were enrolled in this series (17 boys and 53 girls). Mean age was 5.9 years (1.2-13 years). Grade III presented in 37 patients (53%), and 33 patients (47%) were of grade IV. Mean distal UD was 5.5 mm (2.5-13 mm). Mean UDR was 37.8% (18%-70%). Macroplastique injection was performed in all. Subureteric injection was performed in 60 patients (86%), whereas intraureteric injection was performed in 10 patients. No postoperative complications were detected. The effect of grade, UD, and UDR on success after endoscopic injection was tested. UD and UDR were significant predictors of reflux resolution on logistic regression analysis (P <.007 and .001, respectively).

Q: Can distal ureteral diameter predict reflux resolution after endoscopic injection?
A:
chosen: The study tested the predictive value of distal ureteral diameter (UD) on reflux resolution after endoscopic injection in children with primary vesicoureteral reflux (VUR). A retrospective review of 70 patients managed by endoscopic injection was conducted, measuring the largest UD within the false pelvis and calculating the UD ratio (UDR) by dividing UD by the L1-L3 vertebral body distance. Mean distal UD was 5.5 mm, and mean UDR was 37.8%. Logistic regression analysis showed that both UD and UDR were significant predictors of reflux resolution, with P-values of <.007 and .001, respectively. No postoperative complications were detected. The findings indicate that distal ureteral diameter and UDR can predict reflux resolution after endoscopic injection.
rejected: Yes, a **wider distal ureteral diameter** (often measured as >7-8 mm) is associated with **lower success rates** for **vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) resolution** after endoscopic injection (e.g., Deflux). Studies suggest that larger ureteral diameters may indicate poorer ureteral tunnel coaptation, reducing the effectiveness of bulking agents. However, success also depends on other factors like reflux grade, injection technique, and surgeon experience.

Recently, increasing number of literature has identified the posterior tibial slope (PTS) as one of the risk factors of primary anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injury. However, few studies concerning the association between failure of ACL reconstruction (ACLR) and PTS have been published. The objective of this study was to explore the association between the failure of ACLR and PTS at a minimum of two years follow-up.
Two hundred and thirty eight eligible patients from June 2009 to October 2010 were identified from our database. A total of 20 failure cases of ACLR and 20 randomly selected controls were included in this retrospective study. The demographic data and the results of manual maximum side-to-side difference with KT-1000 arthrometer at 30° of knee flexion and pivot-shift test before the ACLR and at the final follow-up were collected. The medial and lateral PTSs were measured using the magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan, based on Hudek's measurement. A comparison of PTS between the two groups was performed.
The overall failure rate of the present study was 8.4%. Of the 40 participants, the mean medial PTS was 4.1° ± 3.2° and the mean lateral PTS was 4.6° ± 2.6°. The medial PTS of the ACLR failure group was significantly steeper than the control group (3.5° ± 2.5° vs. 6.1° ± 2.1°, P = 0.000). Similarly, the lateral PTS of the ACLR failure group was significantly steeper than the control group (2.9° ± 2.1° vs. 5.5° ± 3.0°, P = 0.006). For medial PTS ≥ 5°, the odds ratio of ACLR failure was 6.8 (P = 0.007); for lateral PTS ≥5°, the odds ratio of ACLR failure was 10.8 (P = 0.000).

Q: Are failures of anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction associated with steep posterior tibial slopes?
A:
chosen: The study explored the association between failure of anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction (ACLR) and posterior tibial slope (PTS) at a minimum of two years follow-up. The medial PTS of the ACLR failure group was significantly steeper than the control group (3.5° ± 2.5° vs. 6.1° ± 2.1°, P = 0.000), and the lateral PTS of the ACLR failure group was also significantly steeper than the control group (2.9° ± 2.1° vs. 5.5° ± 3.0°, P = 0.006). For medial PTS ≥ 5°, the odds ratio of ACLR failure was 6.8 (P = 0.007), and for lateral PTS ≥5°, the odds ratio of ACLR failure was 10.8 (P = 0.000). The overall failure rate of the study was 8.4%.
rejected: Yes, failures of anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) reconstruction are associated with steep posterior tibial slopes (PTS). A steeper PTS increases anterior tibial translation and shear forces, which can contribute to graft failure and recurrent instability. Studies suggest that a PTS >12° is a risk factor for ACL reconstruction failure, particularly in patients with high-grade pivot-shift phenomena or revision cases. Addressing PTS through osteotomy may be considered in select cases to improve outcomes.

Surgical excision of ovarian endometriomas in patients desiring pregnancy has recently been criticized because of the risk of damage to healthy ovarian tissue and consequent reduction of ovarian reserve. A correct diagnosis in cases not scheduled for surgery is therefore mandatory in order to avoid unexpected ovarian cancer misdiagnosis. Endometriosis is often associated with high levels of CA125. This marker is therefore not useful for discriminating ovarian endometrioma from ovarian malignancy. The aim of this study was to establish if the serum marker CA72-4 could be helpful in the differential diagnosis between ovarian endometriosis and epithelial ovarian cancer.
Serums CA125 and CA72-4 were measured in 72 patients with ovarian endometriomas and 55 patients with ovarian cancer.
High CA125 concentrations were observed in patients with ovarian endometriosis and in those with ovarian cancer. A marked difference in CA72-4 values was observed between women with ovarian cancer (71.0%) and patients with endometriosis (13.8%) (P<0.0001).

Q: Is CA72-4 a useful biomarker in differential diagnosis between ovarian endometrioma and epithelial ovarian cancer?
A:
chosen: The study aimed to establish if the serum marker CA72-4 could be helpful in the differential diagnosis between ovarian endometriosis and epithelial ovarian cancer, as CA125 is not useful for discriminating ovarian endometrioma from ovarian malignancy due to high levels in both conditions. Serums CA125 and CA72-4 were measured in 72 patients with ovarian endometriomas and 55 patients with ovarian cancer. High CA125 concentrations were observed in patients with ovarian endometriosis and in those with ovarian cancer. A marked difference in CA72-4 values was observed between women with ovarian cancer (71.0%) and patients with endometriosis (13.8%) (P < 0.0001). These results demonstrate that CA72-4 may be a useful biomarker for distinguishing between the two conditions, given the statistically significant difference (P < 0.0001).
rejected: CA72-4 has limited utility in the differential diagnosis between **ovarian endometrioma** and **epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC)**. While it may be elevated in some EOC cases, its sensitivity and specificity are insufficient to reliably distinguish between the two conditions. **CA-125** and imaging (e.g., ultrasound, MRI) remain more clinically useful, often supplemented with other biomarkers (e.g., HE4, ROMA index) for improved accuracy. CA72-4 is not routinely recommended for this purpose.

The effect of preoperative education on anxiety and postoperative outcomes of cardiac surgery patients remains unclear.AIM: The aim of the study was to estimate the effectiveness of a nurse-led preoperative education on anxiety and postoperative outcomes.
A randomised controlled study was designed. All the patients who were admitted for elective cardiac surgery in a general hospital in Athens with knowledge of the Greek language were eligible to take part in the study. Patients in the intervention group received preoperative education by specially trained nurses. The control group received the standard information by the ward personnel. Measurements of anxiety were conducted on admission-A, before surgery-B and before discharge-C by the state-trait anxiety inventory.
The sample consisted of 395 patients (intervention group: 205, control group: 190). The state anxiety on the day before surgery decreased only in the intervention group (34.0 (8.4) versus 36.9 (10.7); P=0.001). The mean decrease in state score during the follow-up period was greater in the intervention group (P=0.001). No significant difference was found in the length of stay or readmission. Lower proportions of chest infection were found in the intervention group (10 (5.3) versus 1 (0.5); P=0.004). Multivariate linear regression revealed that education and score in trait anxiety scale on admission are independent predictors of a reduction in state anxiety.

Q: Can nurse-led preoperative education reduce anxiety and postoperative complications of patients undergoing cardiac surgery?
A:
chosen:
The study aimed to estimate the effectiveness of a nurse-led preoperative education on anxiety and postoperative outcomes in patients undergoing elective cardiac surgery in a general hospital in Athens with knowledge of the Greek language. A randomized controlled trial involved 395 patients (intervention group: 205, control group: 190). The intervention group received preoperative education by specially trained nurses, while the control group received standard information by the ward personnel.  

State anxiety on the day before surgery decreased only in the intervention group (34.0 (8.4) versus 36.9 (10.7); P=0.001). The mean decrease in state score during the follow-up period was greater in the intervention group (P=0.001). No significant difference was found in the length of stay or readmission. Lower proportions of chest infection were found in the intervention group (10 (5.3) versus 1 (0.5); P=0.004). Multivariate linear regression revealed that education and score in trait anxiety scale on admission are independent predictors of a reduction in state anxiety.  

Thus, for patients undergoing elective cardiac surgery, nurse-led preoperative education reduces anxiety and chest infections but not other outcomes like length of stay or readmission.
rejected: Yes, nurse-led preoperative education can reduce anxiety and postoperative complications in patients undergoing cardiac surgery. Studies have shown that structured education by nurses improves patient understanding, lowers preoperative anxiety, and leads to better postoperative recovery, including fewer complications such as infections and shorter hospital stays.

To determine the advantages of scrotal incision in the treatment of undescended testis. Undescended testis is a common pediatric condition and is conventionally managed surgically by orchidopexy. A single scrotal incision orchidopexy has become accepted as a valid approach for patients with palpable undescended testicles. Because this approach also allows easy detection of atrophic testes or testicular remnants, it recently has also emerged as an alternative initial surgical approach to impalpable undescended testicles.
All orchidopexies performed between 2004 and 2008 at our university hospital were prospectively included in this study. A total of 194 scrotal orchidopexies were performed in 154 patients (mean age, 71 months; range, 4-229 months). In all cases a scrotal approach was chosen irrespective of the initial position or presence of an open processus vaginalis. Testicular position was examined at follow-up after a mean period of 10 months (3-22 months).
Overall, 36 of the 46 impalpable testicles (78%) could be diagnosed and treated accordingly, using only a scrotal incision. Conversion to laparoscopy was needed in 4 cases. A limited number of postoperative complications were seen. In all cases, the testes were palpable and remained in the scrotum on follow-up.

Q: Scrotal approach to both palpable and impalpable undescended testes: should it become our first choice?
A:
chosen: A single scrotal incision orchidopexy has become accepted as a valid approach for palpable undescended testicles and has recently emerged as an alternative initial surgical approach to impalpable undescended testicles. The scrotal approach allows easy detection of atrophic testes or testicular remnants. In a study of 194 scrotal orchidopexies performed in 154 patients, 36 of 46 impalpable testicles (78%) could be diagnosed and treated using only a scrotal incision, with conversion to laparoscopy needed in 4 cases. Postoperative complications were limited, and all testes were palpable and remained in the scrotum on follow-up after a mean period of 10 months. The scrotal approach was chosen irrespective of the initial position or presence of an open processus vaginalis.
rejected:
The scrotal approach for both palpable and impalpable undescended testes (UDT) is gaining traction due to its minimally invasive nature, reduced operative time, and comparable success rates to traditional inguinal approaches. However, it should not yet be universally adopted as the first choice for **impalpable testes**, where laparoscopy remains the gold standard for localization and management.  

For **palpable UDT**, the scrotal approach is increasingly favored as a first-line option, provided the surgeon has sufficient experience. For **impalpable testes**, a tailored approach (laparoscopy if non-palpable) remains preferable. Further high-quality studies are needed to solidify its role in all cases.  

**Short answer:** Yes for **palpable** UDT, but not yet for **impalpable** (laparoscopy preferred).

This retrospective study was carried out in the Ear Nose Throat (ENT) Unit of Giannina Gaslini Institute, Genoa, Italy on children operated for adenotonsillectomy (AT) or tonsillectomy (T) between January 2003 and February 2008. We considered in the study all the post-tonsillectomy late haemorrhages irrespective of their severity and for each case we evaluated whether they recurred in the day-time (B) (between 9.00 a.m. and 9.00 p.m.) or in the night-time (A) (between 9.00 p.m. and 9.00 a.m.). Finally we considered the number of haemorrhages per hour in the whole day.
Out of 3306 patients undergoing elective adenotonsillectomy or tonsillectomy, post-operative late haemorrhage occurred in 59 (1.78%). We noted that 42 episodes (71.2%) occurred in the night-time and 17 (28.8%) in the day-time. The average time from the operation was 8.4 days. A statistically significant difference (p=0.002) was found when comparing the frequencies of night-time and day-time haemorrhages. We did not observe any significant difference in the distribution per hour of the haemorrhages.

Q: Post-tonsillectomy late haemorrhage: is it a preferably night-time event?
A:
chosen: The retrospective study conducted at the ENT Unit of Giannina Gaslini Institute found that out of 3306 patients undergoing adenotonsillectomy or tonsillectomy, post-operative late haemorrhage occurred in 59 cases (1.78%). Of these, 42 episodes (71.2%) occurred in the night-time (between 9.00 p.m. and 9.00 a.m.) and 17 (28.8%) in the day-time (between 9.00 a.m. and 9.00 p.m.). A statistically significant difference (p=0.002) was observed between night-time and day-time haemorrhages. The average time from the operation was 8.4 days, and no significant difference was noted in the distribution per hour of the haemorrhages.
rejected: Post-tonsillectomy late hemorrhage (occurring >24 hours after surgery) does not show a clear preference for night-time events. While some studies suggest a slight increase in nocturnal bleeding due to factors like decreased swallowing, dehydration, or blood pressure changes during sleep, the evidence is not conclusive. Most hemorrhages can occur at any time, emphasizing the need for continuous patient vigilance. Always follow clinical guidelines for management.

Multislice helical computed tomography (CT), which can provide detailed 2-D and 3-D reconstructed images, is useful in imaging diagnosis for dental implant treatment. Therefore, in this study, it was performed to clarify the mandibular depiction of double-oblique reconstructed images when changing their thickness.
A total of 38 sites in the mandibular molar region were examined using multislice helical CT. The thicknesses of the double-oblique images using multislice helical CT scans were reconstructed in 4 conditions: 0.3 mm, 0.9 mm, 1.6 mm, and 4.1 mm. In double-oblique images, mandibular depiction was evaluated by 5 oral radiologists using a subjective rating score.
In the alveolar crest and the whole of the mandibular canal, the highest value was obtained with 0.9 mm-thick images; however, there was no significant difference between 0.3 mm and 0.9 mm-thick images.

Q: Can mandibular depiction be improved by changing the thickness of double-oblique computed tomography images?
A:
chosen: Multislice helical computed tomography (CT) was performed to clarify the mandibular depiction of double-oblique reconstructed images when changing their thickness. The thicknesses of the double-oblique images were reconstructed in 4 conditions: 0.3 mm, 0.9 mm, 1.6 mm, and 4.1 mm. In the alveolar crest and the whole of the mandibular canal, the highest value was obtained with 0.9 mm-thick images; however, there was no significant difference between 0.3 mm and 0.9 mm-thick images. The evaluation was performed by 5 oral radiologists using a subjective rating score.
rejected: Yes, adjusting the thickness of double-oblique computed tomography (CT) images can improve mandibular depiction. Thinner slices enhance spatial resolution and reduce partial volume averaging, allowing for better visualization of fine anatomical details, such as the mandibular cortex and trabecular bone. However, excessively thin slices may increase noise, so an optimal balance must be achieved based on clinical needs and imaging protocols.

prompt:
Dickkopf-3 (DKK3) may act as a tumor suppressor as it is down-regulated in various types of cancer. This study assessed the DKK3 protein expression in gastric cancer and its potential value as a prognostic marker.
DKK3 expression was evaluated by immunohistochemistry in 158 gastric cancer samples from patients who underwent gastrectomy from 2002 to 2008. Clinicopathological parameters and survival data were analyzed.
Loss of DKK3 expression was found in 64 of 158 (40.5%) samples, and it was associated with advanced T stage (p<0.001), lymph node metastasis (p<0.001), UICC TNM stage (p<0.001), tumor location (p = 0.029), lymphovascular invasion (p = 0.035), and perineural invasion (p = 0.032). Patients without DKK3 expression in tumor cells had a significantly worse disease-free and overall survival than those with DKK3 expression (p<0.001, and p = 0.001, respectively). TNM stage (p = 0.028 and p<0.001, respectively) and residual tumor (p<0.001 and p = 0.003, respectively) were independent predictors of disease-free and overall survival. Based on the preoperative clinical stage assessed by computed tomography (CT), loss of DKK3 expression was predominantly associated with worse prognosis in patients with clinically node-negative advanced gastric cancer (AGC). The combination of DKK3 expression status and CT increased the accuracy of CT staging for predicting lymph node involvement from 71.5 to 80.0% in AGC patients.

Q: Aberrant loss of dickkopf-3 in gastric cancer: can it predict lymph node metastasis preoperatively?
A:
chosen: Loss of DKK3 expression was found in 40.5% of gastric cancer samples and was associated with lymph node metastasis (p<0.001). Patients without DKK3 expression had significantly worse disease-free and overall survival (p<0.001 and p = 0.001, respectively). Based on preoperative clinical stage assessed by CT, loss of DKK3 expression was predominantly associated with worse prognosis in clinically node-negative advanced gastric cancer (AGC). The combination of DKK3 expression status and CT increased the accuracy of CT staging for predicting lymph node involvement from 71.5 to 80.0% in AGC patients.
rejected:
Aberrant loss of **Dickkopf-3 (DKK3)** in gastric cancer has been associated with **tumor progression and lymph node metastasis (LNM)** in some studies. DKK3, a Wnt signaling antagonist, may act as a tumor suppressor, and its downregulation could promote invasion and metastasis.  

While **preoperative prediction of LNM** in gastric cancer relies primarily on imaging (CT, EUS) and clinical staging, **DKK3 loss** (detected via biopsy or molecular profiling) could serve as a **potential biomarker** to improve risk stratification. However, further validation in large cohorts is needed before it can be routinely used for preoperative prediction.  

**Short answer:** Yes, DKK3 loss may help predict LNM, but it is not yet a standard preoperative tool.

The aim of this study was to analyze the properties of the immune cell microenvironment of regional lymph nodes (LNs) positive for lung cancer.
Twenty-four patients operated on for stages T1 and T2 of the NSCLC, were enrolled in the study. Peripheral blood and LN tissue were obtained from different lymph node sites and levels. As a control, LN tissue was taken from patients diagnosed with emphysema or pneumothorax. The cells from randomly chosen LN were tested by multi-color flow cytometry. Separate portions of LN were snap-frozen and examined for the presence of cytokeratin positive cells (CK). Propensity for apoptosis, level of TCR zeta chain expression of T cells and the number and maturation status of dendritic cells were confronted with the presence of CK-positive cells.
The presence of metastases correlated with the downregulation of TCR zeta, especially CD8(+) T cells. The most striking feature was the reduction in the number of myeloid CD11c(+) dendritic cells in the LN of patients with LN metastases. This could be a reflection of the immunodeficient state observed in lung cancer patients. Even in the absence of metastases in the regional LN, the same type of changes in the LN microenvironment were observed in those LN located nearer the primary tumor.

Q: Can the condition of the cell microenvironment of mediastinal lymph nodes help predict the risk of metastases in non-small cell lung cancer?
A:
chosen: The presence of metastases in non-small cell lung cancer correlated with the downregulation of TCR zeta, especially in CD8(+) T cells, and the most striking feature was the reduction in the number of myeloid CD11c(+) dendritic cells in the lymph nodes of patients with lymph node metastases. These changes could be a reflection of the immunodeficient state observed in lung cancer patients. Even in the absence of metastases in the regional lymph nodes, the same type of changes in the lymph node microenvironment were observed in those lymph nodes located nearer the primary tumor. The study analyzed the properties of the immune cell microenvironment of regional lymph nodes positive for lung cancer, using multi-color flow cytometry and examining cytokeratin positive cells, TCR zeta chain expression, and dendritic cell maturation status.
rejected: Yes, the condition of the cell microenvironment in mediastinal lymph nodes (e.g., immune cell composition, stromal interactions, and molecular signaling) can help predict the risk of metastases in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Studies suggest that features like immunosuppression, tumor-associated fibroblast activity, and angiogenic factors in the lymph node microenvironment may indicate higher metastatic potential. Biomarkers and imaging techniques assessing these factors could improve risk stratification.

prompt:
Esophageal varices are present in 30% to 40% of patients in compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class A) and in 60% to 85% of patients in decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh classes B and C). It is important to identify patients with compensated cirrhosis at risk for esophageal varix development. We evaluated the accuracy of a duplex Doppler ultrasonographic index for predicting the presence or absence of esophageal varices in patients with compensated hepatic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class A) by using endoscopy as the reference standard.
Fifty-six enrolled patients underwent duplex Doppler ultrasonography followed by screening endoscopy. Mean portal vein velocity (PVV), splenic index (SI), splenoportal index (SPI), hepatic and splenic arterial resistive, and pulsatility indices (hepatic artery resistive index [HARI], hepatic artery pulsatility index [HAPI], splenic artery resistive index [SARI], splenic artery pulsatility index [SAPI]) were recorded. Univariate logistic regression analysis was followed by receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve construction for the indices that were significant.
The indices HARI, HAPI, SARI, SAPI were not helpful (p > 0.05). Mean PVV, SI, and SPI were all predictive of the presence of esophageal varices (p < 0.05) and SPI was found to be the most accurate parameter. Of the various cut-off levels of SPI evaluated, a cut-off value of SPI at 5.0, offered the highest diagnostic accuracy (88%). For the 28 patients with SPI<5.0, the absence of esophageal varices in 27 of them could be correctly diagnosed using only SPI without invasive screening endoscopy, with high negative predictive value (96%) and sensitivity (96%). Of the remaining 28 patients with SPI ≥5.0, presence of esophageal varices could be similarly correctly diagnosed in 22 of them by using SPI without screening endoscopy, with high positive predictive value (79%) and specificity (82%).

Q: Is non-invasive diagnosis of esophageal varices in patients with compensated hepatic cirrhosis possible by duplex Doppler ultrasonography?
A:
chosen: The study evaluated the accuracy of duplex Doppler ultrasonography for non-invasive diagnosis of esophageal varices in patients with compensated hepatic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class A), using endoscopy as the reference standard. Fifty-six patients underwent duplex Doppler ultrasonography followed by screening endoscopy. Mean portal vein velocity (PVV), splenic index (SI), and splenoportal index (SPI) were predictive of esophageal varices (p < 0.05), with SPI being the most accurate parameter. A cut-off value of SPI at 5.0 offered the highest diagnostic accuracy (88%). For the 28 patients with SPI<5.0, the absence of esophageal varices was correctly diagnosed in 27 cases without invasive screening endoscopy, with high negative predictive value (96%) and sensitivity (96%). Among the 28 patients with SPI ≥5.0, the presence of esophageal varices was correctly diagnosed in 22 cases without screening endoscopy, with high positive predictive value (79%) and specificity (82%). The indices HARI, HAPI, SARI, and SAPI were not helpful (p > 0.05).
rejected:
Yes, non-invasive diagnosis of esophageal varices (EV) in patients with compensated hepatic cirrhosis is possible using **duplex Doppler ultrasonography**, though with some limitations.  

Studies suggest that Doppler parameters like **portal vein velocity, splenic artery resistive index, and hepatic vein waveforms** may correlate with variceal presence and risk. However, **transient elastography (FibroScan) and platelet count/spleen diameter ratio** are more widely validated for non-invasive screening.  

While duplex Doppler can provide supportive data, **esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) remains the gold standard** for definitive diagnosis.

prompt:
Multiple sclerosis (MS) is the most common chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the central nervous system. The purpose of this study is to determine the relationship between the site of the cervical discopathy and cervical spinal cord plaque in MS patients.
This retrospective study included all patients with a definite diagnosis of MS who were treated at an outpatient clinic between September 2004 and September 2011. All patients underwent cervical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for primary investigation of the disease. Cervical MRI scans were evaluated for detection of any evidence of cervical discopathy and cervical MS plaques. Any correlation between the site of the MS lesions and discopathy was recorded.
From 536 patients who were involved in the study, 214 patients had both cervical discopathy and cervical cord plaques. In this group 148 (69.1% of patients) had cervical plaque at the same site of cervical discopathy. The number of patients with cervical cord plaque and discopathy at same site was significantly higher than those with plaque and discopathy at different sites (P<0.05).

Q: Is there any relation between cervical cord plaques and discopathy in patients with multiple sclerosis?
A:
chosen: In 214 multiple sclerosis (MS) patients with both cervical discopathy and cervical cord plaques, 69.1% had cervical plaques at the same site as the discopathy. The number of patients with cervical cord plaques and discopathy at the same site was significantly higher than those with plaques and discopathy at different sites (P<0.05).
rejected:
Yes, there is a potential relationship between cervical cord plaques and discopathy in patients with **multiple sclerosis (MS)**.  

- **Cervical cord plaques** (demyelinating lesions) in MS can lead to **spinal cord atrophy** and **axonal damage**, which may exacerbate or mimic symptoms of **cervical discopathy** (degenerative disc disease).  
- **Mechanical stress** from discopathy (e.g., herniation, stenosis) could worsen **MS-related cord damage** due to impaired blood flow or increased vulnerability of demyelinated areas.  
- **Diagnostic overlap** may occur, as both conditions can cause myelopathy (e.g., weakness, sensory deficits), complicating clinical assessment.  

While they are distinct pathologies, their coexistence may contribute to **worse neurological outcomes** in MS patients. Further research is needed to clarify causal interactions.

Bias against operating on patients with prosthetic valve endocarditis (PVE) who have multiple prostheses may preclude the use of life-saving valve replacement. We investigated the accuracy of the preoperative diagnosis of PVE in patients with both mitral and aortic prosthesis and the safety of single-valve replacement when only one valve seemed infected.
Patients with a diagnosis of active PVE who had mitral and aortic prosthesis in place were assessed. We looked at the methods for diagnosis, causative agents, indication for valve replacement, operative findings and outcome.
Twenty patients, who had both mitral and aortic prostheses and a diagnosis of PVE, were assessed. Streptococci and staphylococci caused 70% of cases. By means of echocardiography, the valves involved were: mitral (11 patients), aortic (six patients), and in three cases both prosthetic valves seemed infected. Surgery was undertaken in 17 patients (85%). The positive predictive value of transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) for the preoperative diagnosis of the site of infection was 100%. In 13 patients, only the prosthetic valve that seemed infected was replaced. Four of these patients died within a week after the procedure. Nine patients survived the surgical procedure, completed a course of antimicrobial therapy and were followed up for 15.78 months (95% CI: 12.83-18.72). All were considered cured and relapses were not observed.

Echocardiography identified the valves involved as mitral in 11 patients, aortic in six, and both in three cases. Surgery was performed in 17 patients (85%). The positive predictive value of transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) for preoperative diagnosis of the infection site was 100%. In 13 patients, only the prosthetic valve that seemed infected was replaced. Four of these patients died within a week post-procedure. Nine patients survived surgery, completed antimicrobial therapy, and were followed for 15.78 months (95% CI: 12.83-18.72). All were considered cured with no relapses observed.
rejected: In patients with double prosthetic valve endocarditis (PVE), single-valve replacement may be considered in select cases (e.g., localized infection, low surgical risk), but **complete removal and replacement of both infected prostheses is generally recommended** to reduce recurrence and improve survival. Single-valve replacement carries a higher risk of persistent infection and worse outcomes compared to double-valve replacement. The decision should be individualized based on intraoperative findings, microbiology, and patient factors.

The aim of the present study was to explore patients' views on the acceptability and feasibility of using colour to describe osteoarthritis (OA) pain, and whether colour could be used to communicate pain to healthcare professionals.
Six group interviews were conducted with 17 patients with knee OA. Discussion topics included first impressions about using colour to describe pain, whether participants could associate their pain with colour, how colours related to changes to intensity and different pain qualities, and whether they could envisage using colour to describe pain to healthcare professionals.
The group interviews indicated that, although the idea of using colour was generally acceptable, it did not suit all participants as a way of describing their pain. The majority of participants chose red to describe high-intensity pain; the reasons given were because red symbolized inflammation, fire, anger and the stop signal in a traffic light system. Colours used to describe the absence of pain were chosen because of their association with positive emotional feelings, such as purity, calmness and happiness. A range of colours was chosen to represent changes in pain intensity. Aching pain was consistently identified as being associated with colours such as grey or black, whereas sharp pain was described using a wider selection of colours. The majority of participants thought that they would be able to use colour to describe their pain to healthcare professionals, although issues around the interpretability and standardization of colour were raised.

To determine whether TDP-43 type is associated with distinct patterns of brain atrophy on MRI in subjects with pathologically confirmed frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD).

In each case we applied published criteria for subclassification of FTLD-TDP into FTLD-TDP types 1-3. Voxel-based morphometry was used to compare subjects with each of the different FTLD-TDP types to age- and gender-matched normal controls (n = 30). We also assessed different pathologic and genetic variants within, and across, the different types.
Twenty-two subjects were classified as FTLD-TDP type 1, 9 as type 2, and 11 as type 3. We identified different patterns of atrophy across the types with type 1 showing frontotemporal and parietal atrophy, type 2 predominantly anterior temporal lobe atrophy, and type 3 predominantly posterior frontal atrophy. Within the FTLD-TDP type 1 group, those with a progranulin mutation had significantly more lateral temporal lobe atrophy than those without. All type 2 subjects were diagnosed with semantic dementia. Subjects with a pathologic diagnosis of FTLD with motor neuron degeneration had a similar pattern of atrophy, regardless of whether they were type 1 or type 3.

Q: Does TDP-43 type confer a distinct pattern of atrophy in frontotemporal lobar degeneration?
A:

rejected:
Yes, TDP-43 pathology in frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD-TDP) is associated with distinct patterns of atrophy that vary by subtype (A, B, C, D, and E). For example:

- **Type B**: Widespread frontal and temporal involvement, often symmetric.  
- **Type C**: Prominent anterior temporal lobe atrophy, typically asymmetric.  

These patterns correlate with clinical phenotypes (e.g., behavioral variant FTD, semantic variant PPA) and help differentiate FTLD-TDP from other proteinopathies like tauopathies.

Receptor subtype NmUR1, but not NmUR2, was expressed in the endocrine pancreas, predominantly in the periphery. Cyclosomatostatin increased insulin secretion and reduced NmU-induced inhibition of insulin secretion.

Q: Does somatostatin confer insulinostatic effects of neuromedin u in the rat pancreas?
A:
chosen: Neuromedin U (NmU) reduces insulin secretion from isolated pancreatic islets and in situ perfused rat pancreas. rejected: Yes, somatostatin mediates the insulinostatic effects of neuromedin U (NMU) in the rat pancreas. The aim of the present study was to assess the effects of exercise training on heart rate, QT interval, and on the relation between ventricular repolarization and heart rate in men and women.
A 24 h Holter recording was obtained in 80 healthy subjects (40 males) who differed for the degree of physical activity. Trained individuals showed a lower heart rate and a higher heart rate variability than sedentary subjects, independent of the gender difference in basal heart rate. Mean 24 h QTc was similar in trained and non-trained men, while a significant difference was observed between trained and non-trained women. Exercise training reduced the QT/RR slope in both genders. This effect on the QT/RR relation was more marked in women; in fact, the gender difference in the ventricular repolarization duration at low heart rate observed in sedentary subjects was no longer present among trained individuals.

Q: Effects of exercise training on heart rate and QT interval in healthy young individuals: are there gender differences?

The gender difference in ventricular repolarization duration at low heart rate observed in sedentary subjects was no longer present among trained individuals.
rejected:
Exercise training generally reduces resting heart rate (HR) and shortens the QT interval (corrected for HR, QTc) in healthy young individuals due to improved cardiac efficiency and autonomic tone. However, **gender differences** exist:  

- **Women** tend to have higher resting HR and longer QTc intervals at baseline compared to men.  
- After training, **men** typically show greater reductions in HR, while **women** may exhibit less pronounced QT interval shortening, possibly due to hormonal influences (e.g., estrogen’s effect on ion channels).  

Thus, while both genders benefit, the magnitude of adaptation differs, with men showing more significant HR reductions and women having inherently longer QTc intervals that may respond differently to training.

The purpose of our study was to determine the effectiveness, clinical impact, and feasibility of double reading barium enemas.
Independent double readings of 1,003 consecutive barium enemas (822 double- and 181 single-contrast examinations) were prospectively performed. From this pool of 1,003 examinations, 994 were included in our study. Examinations showing at least one polyp or carcinoma 5 mm or larger were considered to have positive results. For combined readings, results were considered positive if either of the two interpreters reported finding a polyp or carcinoma. A McNemar test was used to compare the first reader's results with the combined results of the first and second readers. Results were retrospectively correlated with endoscopic or surgical results in 360 patients, and agreement between first and combined readings and endoscopic results was determined.
Adding a second reader increased the number of positive results on examinations from 249 to 315 (p<0.0001) and resulted in potential alteration of clinical treatment in 98 patients (9.9%). Sensitivity of the first and combined readings for detection of all lesions was identical, 76.3% (95% CI, 65.4-87.1%). Specificity decreased from 91.0% (95% CI, 87.9-94.3%) for the first reading to 86.4% (95% CI, 82.2-90.0%) for the combined reading. The overall measurement of agreement decreased from a kappa value of 61.8 (95% CI, 51.2-72.4%) for the first reading to 52.9 (95% CI, 42.2-63.6%) for the combined reading. The second reading required an average of 3.3 min. Sensitivity for the detection of adenocarcinomas was 100%.

Q: Double reading of barium enemas: is it necessary?
A:
chosen: The study determined the effectiveness, clinical impact, and feasibility of double reading barium enemas by prospectively performing independent double readings of 1,003 consecutive barium enemas (822 double- and 181 single-contrast examinations), with 994 included in the analysis. Adding a second reader increased the number of positive results from 249 to 315, a statistically significant difference (p<0.0001), and potentially altered clinical treatment in 98 patients (9.9%). The sensitivity of the first and combined readings for detecting all lesions was identical at 76.3%, while specificity decreased from 91.0% for the first reading to 86.4% for the combined reading. The overall agreement decreased from a kappa value of 61.8 for the first reading to 52.9 for the combined reading. The second reading required an average of 3.3 minutes, and sensitivity for detecting adenocarcinomas was 100%. Examinations were considered positive if either interpreter reported a polyp or carcinoma 5 mm or larger, and results were retrospectively correlated with endoscopic or surgical findings in 360 patients.
rejected:
Double reading of barium enemas is not routinely necessary but may be beneficial in specific cases where initial interpretation is uncertain or for quality assurance in teaching settings. Studies suggest that double reading improves detection rates for subtle lesions (e.g., polyps or early cancers), but the added time and cost may not justify universal implementation. Selective use, particularly for high-risk patients or complex cases, is a more practical approach.  

(Answer based on radiology literature and consensus guidelines.)

The objective of the current study is to determine to what extent the reduction of Chile's traffic fatalities and injuries during 2000-2012 was related to the police traffic enforcement increment registered after the introduction of its 2005 traffic law reform.

Dependent variables were traffic fatality and severe injury rates per population and vehicle fleet. Oil prices, alcohol consumption, proportion of male population 15-24 years old, unemployment, road infrastructure investment, years' effects and regions' effects represented control variables.
Empirical estimates from instrumental variables suggest that the enactment of the traffic law reform in interaction with number of traffic tickets per police officer is significantly associated with a decrease of 8% in traffic fatalities and 7% in severe injuries. The International Association of the Diabetes and Pregnancy Study Groups (IADPSG) recently recommended new criteria for diagnosing gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM). This study was undertaken to determine whether adopting the IADPSG criteria would be cost-effective, compared with the current standard of care.
We developed a decision analysis model comparing the cost-utility of three strategies to identify GDM: 1) no screening, 2) current screening practice (1-h 50-g glucose challenge test between 24 and 28 weeks followed by 3-h 100-g glucose tolerance test when indicated), or 3) screening practice proposed by the IADPSG. Assumptions included that 1) women diagnosed with GDM received additional prenatal monitoring, mitigating the risks of preeclampsia, shoulder dystocia, and birth injury; and 2) GDM women had opportunity for intensive postdelivery counseling and behavior modification to reduce future diabetes risks. The primary outcome measure was the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER).
Our model demonstrates that the IADPSG recommendations are cost-effective only when postdelivery care reduces diabetes incidence. For every 100,000 women screened, 6,178 quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) are gained, at a cost of $125,633,826. The ICER for the IADPSG strategy compared with the current standard was $20,336 per QALY gained. When postdelivery care was not accomplished, the IADPSG strategy was no longer cost-effective. These results were robust in sensitivity analyses.

Q: Screening for gestational diabetes mellitus: are the criteria proposed by the international association of the Diabetes and Pregnancy Study Groups cost-effective?
A:
chosen:

The model assumed that women diagnosed with GDM received additional prenatal monitoring, mitigating the risks of preeclampsia, shoulder dystocia, and birth injury, and had opportunity for intensive postdelivery counseling and behavior modification to reduce future diabetes risks.  

The IADPSG recommendations were cost-effective only when postdelivery care reduced diabetes incidence. For every 100,000 women screened, 6,178 quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) were gained, at a cost of $125,633,826. The ICER for the IADPSG strategy compared with the current standard was $20,336 per QALY gained. When postdelivery care was not accomplished, the IADPSG strategy was no longer cost-effective. These results were robust in sensitivity analyses.

A variable effect of inflammation on alloimmunization to transfused red blood cells (RBCs) in mice has been recently reported. We investigated whether RBC alloimmunization in humans was affected by transfusion of blood products in temporal proximity to experiencing a febrile transfusion reaction (FTR) to platelets (PLTs), an event predominantly mediated by inflammatory cytokines.
Blood bank databases were used to identify patients who experienced an FTR or possible FTR to PLTs from August 2000 to March 2008 (FTR group). The control group of patients received a PLT transfusion on randomly selected dates without experiencing an FTR. The "event" was defined as the PLT transfusion that caused the FTR in the FTR group or the index PLT transfusion in the control group. The number of transfused blood products and their proximity to the event were recorded along with other recipient data. The primary endpoint was the rate of RBC alloimmunization between the two groups.
There were 190 recipients in the FTR group and 245 in the control group. Overall, the recipients in the control group were younger and received more blood products on the day of their event and over the subsequent 10 days. The alloimmunization rate among recipients in the FTR group was higher than in the control group (8% vs. 3%, respectively; p = 0.026).

Q: Does a febrile reaction to platelets predispose recipients to red blood cell alloimmunization?
A:
chosen: A variable effect of inflammation on alloimmunization to transfused red blood cells (RBCs) in mice has been recently reported. The study investigated whether RBC alloimmunization in humans was affected by transfusion of blood products in temporal proximity to experiencing a febrile transfusion reaction (FTR) to platelets (PLTs), an event predominantly mediated by inflammatory cytokines. The FTR group consisted of 190 recipients who experienced an FTR or possible FTR to PLTs, while the control group had 245 recipients who received a PLT transfusion without experiencing an FTR. The alloimmunization rate among recipients in the FTR group was higher than in the control group (8% vs. 3%, respectively; p = 0.026).
rejected:
Current evidence does not strongly support that febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions (FNHTRs) to platelet transfusions directly predispose recipients to red blood cell (RBC) alloimmunization. While inflammation and immune activation during transfusion reactions could theoretically enhance alloimmunization risk, studies have not consistently demonstrated a significant association between FNHTRs and subsequent RBC antibody formation.  

However, alloimmunization is more strongly linked to factors such as recipient immune status, underlying conditions, and exposure to mismatched RBC antigens. Further research is needed to clarify any potential relationship.  

**Short Answer:** No, current evidence does not clearly show that febrile reactions to platelets increase RBC alloimmunization risk.

Adoption and implementation of evidence-based measures for catheter care leads to reductions in central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) rates in the NICU. The purpose of this study is to evaluate whether this rate reduction is sustainable for at least 1 year and to identify key determinants of this sustainability at the NICU of the Floating Hospital for Children at Tufts Medical Center.
We reviewed the incidence of CLABSIs in the NICU temporally to the implementation of new practice policies and procedures, from July 2008 to December 2013.
Adoption of standardized care practices, including bundles and checklists, was associated with a significant reduction of the CLABSI rate to zero for>370 consecutive days in our NICU in 2012. Overall, our CLABSI rates decreased from 4.1 per 1000 line days in 2009 (13 infections; 3163 line days) to 0.94 in 2013 (2 infections; 2115 line days), which represents a 77% reduction over a 5-year period. In the first quarter of 2013, there was a brief increase in CLABSI rate to 3.3 per 1000 line days; after a series of interventions, the CLABSI rate was maintained at zero for>600 days. Ongoing training, surveillance, and vigilance with catheter insertion and maintenance practices and improved documentation were identified as key drivers for success.

Q: Is zero central line-associated bloodstream infection rate sustainable?

chosen: The study at the NICU of the Floating Hospital for Children at Tufts Medical Center demonstrated that adoption of standardized care practices, including bundles and checklists, was associated with a significant reduction of the CLABSI rate to zero for over 370 consecutive days in 2012. After a brief increase in the first quarter of 2013, the CLABSI rate was maintained at zero for over 600 days following interventions. Ongoing training, surveillance, vigilance with catheter insertion and maintenance practices, and improved documentation were identified as key drivers for success. These findings suggest that a zero CLABSI rate is sustainable for extended periods with consistent implementation of evidence-based measures.
rejected:
Achieving a **sustained zero central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) rate** is theoretically possible but extremely challenging in practice. It requires **rigorous adherence** to evidence-based prevention protocols (e.g., sterile insertion, proper maintenance, hand hygiene, prompt line removal), a strong safety culture, continuous surveillance, and staff accountability. While some healthcare facilities have reported prolonged periods without CLABSIs, sustaining **zero indefinitely** is difficult due to human factors, system vulnerabilities, and the complexity of patient care. However, **near-zero rates** are a realistic and worthy goal through relentless improvement efforts.

To investigate the effect of bracket-ligature combination on the amount of orthodontic space closure over three months.
Randomized clinical trial with three parallel groups.

Forty-five patients requiring upper first premolar extractions.
Informed consent was obtained and participants were randomly allocated into one of three groups: (1) conventional pre-adjusted edgewise brackets and elastomeric ligatures; (2) conventional pre-adjusted edgewise brackets and Super Slick(®) low friction elastomeric ligatures; (3) Damon 3MX(®) passive self-ligating brackets. Space closure was undertaken on 0·019×0·025-inch stainless steel archwires with nickel-titanium coil springs. Participants were recalled at four weekly intervals. The amount of space closure was very similar between the three groups (1 mm per 28 days); however, there was a wide variation in the rate of space closure between individuals. The differences in the amount of space closure over three months between the three groups was very small and non-significant (P = 0·718).

Q: Does the bracket-ligature combination affect the amount of orthodontic space closure over three months?
A:
chosen: The study investigated the effect of bracket-ligature combination on the amount of orthodontic space closure over three months in forty-five patients requiring upper first premolar extractions. A one-way ANOVA analysis showed the amount of space closure was very similar between the three groups (1 mm per 28 days), with a wide variation in the rate of space closure between individuals. The differences in the amount of space closure over three months between the groups were very small and non-significant (P = 0·718).
rejected: Current evidence suggests that the bracket-ligature combination may influence the rate of orthodontic space closure, but the effect over three months is generally modest. Studies indicate that self-ligating brackets (especially passive types) and elastic ligatures may allow slightly faster initial tooth movement compared to conventional ligatures, but the difference is often not clinically significant in such a short period. Factors like force magnitude, archwire type, and patient biology play larger roles. More high-quality research is needed for definitive conclusions.

The precise correction of refractive error is especially important in young adults. It is unclear whether cycloplegic refraction is necessary in this age group. The purpose of this study was to compare the non-cycloplegic and cycloplegic spherical equivalent (SE) refractive error measured in young adults.
This was a prospective study of 1400 eyes (n = 700) of enlisted soldiers aged 18 to 21 years who were consecutively evaluated in an outpatient army ophthalmology clinic. One drop of cyclopentolate 1 % was installed twice 10 min apart, and cycloplegic refraction was performed in both eyes 40 min later using an auto-refractor. The difference between non-cycloplegic and cycloplegic refractive measurements was analyzed.
The mean difference in SE between non-cycloplegic and cycloplegic measurements was 0.68 ± 0.83 D (95 % CI, 0.64-0.72). Significantly greater differences were observed in hypermetropes than myopes (1.30 ± 0.90 D versus 0.46 ± 0.68 D, p < 0.001). Moderate hypermetropes (2 to 5 D) demonstrated significantly greater refractive error than mild (0.5 to 2 D) or severe (>5 D) hypermetropes (1.71 ± 1.18 D versus 1.19 ± 0.74 D and 1.16 ± 1.08 D respectively, p < 0.001).

Q: Cycloplegic autorefraction in young adults: is it mandatory?
A:
chosen: The study compared non-cycloplegic and cycloplegic spherical equivalent refractive error in young adults aged 18 to 21 years, finding a mean difference of 0.68 ± 0.83 D (95% CI, 0.64-0.72). Hypermetropes showed significantly greater differences than myopes (1.30 ± 0.90 D versus 0.46 ± 0.68 D, p < 0.001). Moderate hypermetropes (2 to 5 D) demonstrated significantly greater refractive error than mild (0.5 to 2 D) or severe (>5 D) hypermetropes (1.71 ± 1.18 D versus 1.19 ± 0.74 D and 1.16 ± 1.08 D respectively, p < 0.001). The study involved 1400 eyes of 700 enlisted soldiers, with cycloplegic refraction performed using an auto-refractor after administering cyclopentolate 1%. The purpose was to evaluate whether cycloplegic refraction is necessary in this age group.
rejected:
Cycloplegic autorefraction is not strictly mandatory in young adults but is often recommended, especially in cases of suspected accommodative spasm, high refractive error, or inconsistent subjective refraction results. It provides a more accurate assessment by paralyzing accommodation, reducing overestimation of myopia or underestimation of hyperopia. However, in cooperative patients with reliable subjective refraction, non-cycloplegic measurements may suffice. Clinical judgment and patient-specific factors should guide the decision.  

(Answer kept brief as requested.)

rejected:
Yes, changes in the serum levels of **IL-2, IL-4, TNF-α, and IL-6** can reflect inflammatory activity in patients with **post-ERCP pancreatitis (PEP)**.  

- **IL-6** is a well-established marker of acute inflammation and is significantly elevated in PEP, correlating with disease severity.  
- **TNF-α** is a pro-inflammatory cytokine that contributes to the early inflammatory response in pancreatitis.  
- **IL-2** (pro-inflammatory) and **IL-4** (anti-inflammatory) may also show altered levels, but their roles are less consistently reported compared to IL-6 and TNF-α.

Two common causes of cervical myelopathy include degenerative stenosis and ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL). It has been postulated that patients with OPLL have more complications and worse outcomes than those with degenerative stenosis. The authors sought to compare the surgical results of laminoplasty in the treatment of cervical stenosis with myelopathy due to either degenerative changes or segmental OPLL.
The authors conducted a retrospective review of 40 instrumented laminoplasty cases performed at a single institution over a 4-year period to treat cervical myelopathy without kyphosis. Twelve of these patients had degenerative cervical stenotic myelopathy ([CSM]; degenerative group), and the remaining 28 had segmental OPLL (OPLL group). The 2 groups had statistically similar demographic characteristics and number of treated levels (mean 3.9 surgically treated levels; p>0.05). The authors collected perioperative and follow-up data, including radiographic results.
The overall clinical follow-up rate was 88%, and the mean clinical follow-up duration was 16.4 months. The mean radiographic follow-up rate was 83%, and the mean length of radiographic follow-up was 9.3 months. There were no significant differences in the estimated blood loss (EBL) or length of hospital stay (LOS) between the groups (p>0.05). The mean EBL and LOS for the degenerative group were 206 ml and 3.7 days, respectively. The mean EBL and LOS for the OPLL group were 155 ml and 4 days, respectively. There was a statistically significant improvement of more than one grade in the Nurick score for both groups following surgery (p<0.05). The Nurick score improvement was not statistically different between the groups (p>0.05). The visual analog scale (VAS) neck pain scores were similar between groups pre- and postoperatively (p>0.05). The complication rates were not statistically different between groups either (p>0.05). Radiographically, both groups lost extension range of motion (ROM) following laminoplasty, but this change was not statistically significant (p>0.05).

Q: Laminoplasty outcomes: is there a difference between patients with degenerative stenosis and those with ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament?
A:
chosen:
The authors conducted a retrospective review of 40 instrumented laminoplasty cases to compare outcomes between 12 patients with degenerative cervical stenotic myelopathy (CSM) and 28 patients with segmental ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL). The 2 groups had statistically similar demographic characteristics and number of treated levels (mean 3.9 surgically treated levels; p>0.05).  

The overall clinical follow-up rate was 88%, with a mean clinical follow-up duration of 16.4 months. The mean radiographic follow-up rate was 83%, with a mean radiographic follow-up length of 9.3 months. There were no significant differences in estimated blood loss (EBL) or length of hospital stay (LOS) between the groups (p>0.05). The degenerative group had a mean EBL of 206 ml and LOS of 3.7 days, while the OPLL group had a mean EBL of 155 ml and LOS of 4 days.  

Both groups showed a statistically significant improvement of more than one grade in the Nurick score postoperatively (p<0.05), with no significant difference between groups (p>0.05). The visual analog scale (VAS) neck pain scores were similar pre- and postoperatively (p>0.05), and complication rates were not statistically different (p>0.05). Radiographically, both groups lost extension range of motion (ROM) after laminoplasty, but this change was not statistically significant (p>0.05).  

The authors reported no significant differences in surgical outcomes between patients with degenerative stenosis and those with segmental OPLL.
rejected:
Yes, there are differences in laminoplasty outcomes between patients with degenerative cervical stenosis (DCS) and those with ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL).  

- **OPLL patients** tend to have worse surgical outcomes, including higher rates of complications (e.g., C5 palsy), less neurological recovery, and a greater risk of late neurological deterioration due to disease progression.  
- **DCS patients** generally show better neurological improvement, fewer complications, and more stable long-term results.  

OPLL is a progressive disease, which may contribute to poorer long-term outcomes compared to DCS.

Although the Internet is commonly used to recruit samples in studies of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-related risk behaviors, it has not been used to measure patient-reported well-being. As the burden of long-term chronic HIV infection rises, the Internet may offer enormous potential for recruitment to research and interventions.
This study aimed to compare two samples of gay men living with HIV, one recruited via the Web and the other recruited in outpatient settings, in terms of self-reported physical and psychological symptom burden.
The Internet sample was recruited from a UK-wide Web-based survey of gay men with diagnosed HIV. Of these, 154 respondents identified themselves as resident in London and were included in this analysis. The HIV clinic sample was recruited from five HIV outpatient clinics. Of these participants, 400 gay men recruited in London clinics were included in this analysis.
The Web-based sample was younger than the clinic sample (37.3 years, SD 7.0 vs 40.9 years, SD 8.3), more likely to be in paid employment (72.8%, 99/136 vs 60.1%, 227/378), less likely to be on antiretroviral therapy (ART) (58.4%, 90/154 vs 68.0%, 266/391), and had worse mean psychological symptom burden compared to the clinic sample (mean scores: 1.61, SD 1.09 vs 1.36, SD 0.96) but similar physical symptom burden (mean scores: 0.78, SD 0.65 vs 0.70, SD 0.74). In multivariable logistic regression, for the physical symptom burden model, adjusted for age, ethnicity, employment status, and ART use, the recruitment setting (ie, Web-based vs clinic) was not significantly associated with high physical symptom score. The only variable that remained significantly associated with high physical symptom score was employment status, with those in employment being less likely to report being in the upper (worst) physical symptom tertile versus the other two tertiles (adjusted OR 0.41, 95% CI 0.28-0.62, P<.001). For the psychological symptom burden model, those recruited via the Web were significantly more likely to report being in the upper (worst) tertile (adjusted OR 2.20, 95% CI 1.41-3.44, P=.001). In addition, those in employment were less likely to report being in the upper (worst) psychological symptom tertile compared to those not in employment (adjusted OR 0.32, 95% CI 0.21-0.49, P<.001).

Q: Do Web-based and clinic samples of gay men living with HIV differ on self-reported physical and psychological symptoms?
A:
chosen:
The study compared two samples of gay men living with HIV—one recruited via a UK-wide Web-based survey (154 London residents) and the other from five HIV outpatient clinics (400 London residents)—to assess differences in self-reported physical and psychological symptom burden.  

The Web-based sample was younger (37.3 years, SD 7.0 vs 40.9 years, SD 8.3), more likely to be in paid employment (72.8% vs 60.1%), and less likely to be on antiretroviral therapy (58.4% vs 68.0%) compared to the clinic sample. The Web-based sample reported worse mean psychological symptom burden (1.61, SD 1.09 vs 1.36, SD 0.96) but similar physical symptom burden (0.78, SD 0.65 vs 0.70, SD 0.74).  

In multivariable logistic regression adjusted for age, ethnicity, employment status, and ART use, recruitment setting (Web-based vs clinic) was not significantly associated with being in the upper (worst) physical symptom tertile. The only variable significantly associated with being in the upper physical symptom tertile was employment status, with those in employment being less likely to report being in the upper tertile versus the other two tertiles (adjusted OR 0.41, 95% CI 0.28-0.62, P<.001). For psychological symptom burden, those recruited via the Web were significantly more likely to report being in the upper (worst) tertile (adjusted OR 2.20, 95% CI 1.41-3.44, P=.001). Those in employment were less likely to report being in the upper psychological symptom tertile compared to those not in employment (adjusted OR 0.32, 95% CI 0.21-0.49, P<.001).
rejected:
Research suggests that web-based and clinic samples of gay men living with HIV may differ in self-reported physical and psychological symptoms. Web-based samples often report higher levels of psychological distress (e.g., depression, anxiety) and sometimes more physical symptoms, possibly due to greater anonymity encouraging disclosure or selection bias (e.g., those with more severe symptoms seeking online support). Clinic samples, while more representative of the general HIV-positive population, may underreport symptoms due to social desirability bias or reliance on clinical assessments. However, findings vary by study, and methodological differences (e.g., recruitment, measurement tools) can influence results.  

For a precise answer, consult specific studies comparing these sampling methods in HIV populations. Let me know if you'd like references!

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a primary degenerative disease that progressively affects all brain functions, with devastating consequences for the patient, the patient's family and society. Rest regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) could have a strategic role in differentiating between AD patients and normal controls, but its use for this purpose has a low discriminatory capacity. The purpose of this study was to evaluate whether the diagnostic sensitivity of rCBF single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) could be increased by using an episodic memory task provocation, i.e. memory-provoked rCBF-SPECT (MP-SPECT).
Eighteen persons (73.2+/-4.8 years) with mild AD and 18 healthy elderly (69.4+/-3.9 years) were included in the study. The subjects were injected with (99m)Tc-hexamethylpropylene amine oxime (HMPAO) during memory provocation with faces and names, followed by an rCBF-SPECT study. The rCBF (99m)Tc-HMPAO SPECT images were analysed using statistical parametric mapping (SPM2). Peaks with a false discovery rate corrected value of 0.05 were considered significant.
On MP-SPECT, the AD group showed a significant rCBF reduction in the left parietal cortex in comparison with healthy elderly. At rest, no significant group differences were seen.

Q: Memory-provoked rCBF-SPECT as a diagnostic tool in Alzheimer's disease?
A:
chosen: The study evaluated whether memory-provoked rCBF-SPECT (MP-SPECT) could increase the diagnostic sensitivity of rCBF single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) in Alzheimer's disease (AD) by using an episodic memory task provocation. Eighteen mild AD patients and 18 healthy elderly were injected with (99m)Tc-hexamethylpropylene amine oxime (HMPAO) during memory provocation with faces and names, followed by an rCBF-SPECT study. The images were analyzed using statistical parametric mapping (SPM2), with peaks at a false discovery rate corrected value of 0.05 considered significant. On MP-SPECT, the AD group showed a significant rCBF reduction in the left parietal cortex compared to healthy elderly, while no significant group differences were observed at rest. This suggests MP-SPECT may improve discriminatory capacity over rest rCBF-SPECT in AD diagnosis.
rejected:
Memory-provoked **regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) SPECT** can be a useful diagnostic tool in **Alzheimer’s disease (AD)** by detecting **hypoperfusion patterns** in key brain regions (e.g., temporoparietal cortex and posterior cingulate) during memory tasks. Compared to resting-state SPECT, memory activation may enhance sensitivity by revealing functional deficits early in AD. However, its clinical use is limited by variability, lack of standardization, and the broader adoption of **PET (e.g., amyloid or FDG-PET)** and CSF biomarkers for definitive AD diagnosis. SPECT remains an alternative where PET is unavailable.  

**Short answer:** Yes, it can help detect AD-related hypoperfusion patterns under memory tasks, but it is less standardized and less commonly used than PET or biomarkers.

Although body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) is classified in DSM-III-R as a nonpsychotic somatoform disorder, controversy exists as to whether BDD can present with psychotic features. If it can, this raises the possibility that its DSM-III-R psychotic counterpart-delusional disorder, somatic type--may not be a separate disorder. The purpose of this study was to determine whether patients with nonpsychotic BDD (defined according to DSM-III-R criteria, i.e., with maintenance of some insight) were different from patients with psychotic BDD (those whose preoccupation was without insight and of delusional intensity).
Fifty consecutive patients meeting DSM-III-R criteria A and C for BDD were assessed with a semistructured interview and the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-III-R (SCID). Family histories of psychiatric disorders were blindly assessed. The 24 patients with nonpsychotic BDD were compared with the 26 patients with psychotic BDD with respect to demographics, phenomenology, course of illness, associated features, comorbid psychiatric disorders, family history, and treatment response.
Patients with psychotic BDD displayed a significantly higher rate of lifetime DSM-III-R psychotic disorder diagnoses than patients with nonpsychotic BDD. However, the two groups did not differ significantly on most other variables examined. For instance, both psychotic and nonpsychotic patients displayed significant morbidity; high comorbidity with mood, anxiety, and psychoactive substance use disorders; and apparent preferential response to serotonin reuptake inhibitors rather than to non-serotonin reuptake blocking antidepressants or antipsychotics.

Q: Body dysmorphic disorder: does it have a psychotic subtype?
A:
chosen: Body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) can present with psychotic features, raising the possibility that its DSM-III-R psychotic counterpart, delusional disorder somatic type, may not be a separate disorder. The study compared 24 patients with nonpsychotic BDD (defined by DSM-III-R criteria with maintenance of some insight) and 26 patients with psychotic BDD (those whose preoccupation was without insight and of delusional intensity). Patients with psychotic BDD displayed a significantly higher rate of lifetime DSM-III-R psychotic disorder diagnoses than nonpsychotic BDD patients. However, the two groups did not differ significantly on most other variables, including morbidity, high comorbidity with mood, anxiety, and psychoactive substance use disorders, and preferential response to serotonin reuptake inhibitors over non-serotonin reuptake blocking antidepressants or antipsychotics.
rejected: Yes, Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD) can have a psychotic subtype, characterized by **delusional beliefs** about perceived defects in appearance. In such cases, the individual is completely convinced of their distorted self-perception, with no insight into the irrationality of their beliefs. This is classified as **BDD with absent insight/delusional beliefs** in the DSM-5, which acknowledges the overlap between severe BDD and psychotic disorders. However, BDD itself is not classified as a psychotic disorder but may include psychotic features in extreme cases.

The purpose of this study was to investigate whether knowledge of ultrasound-obtained estimated fetal weight (US-EFW) is a risk factor for cesarean delivery (CD).
Retrospective cohort from a single center in 2009-2010 of singleton, term live births. CD rates were compared for women with and without US-EFW within 1 month of delivery and adjusted for potential confounders.
Of the 2329 women in our cohort, 50.2% had US-EFW within 1 month of delivery. CD was significantly more common for women with US-EFW (15.7% vs 10.2%; P<.001); after we controlled for confounders, US-EFW remained an independent risk factor for CD (odds ratio, 1.44; 95% confidence interval, 1.1-1.9). The risk increased when US-EFW was>3500 g (odds ratio, 1.8; 95% confidence interval, 1.3-2.7).

Q: Estimated fetal weight by ultrasound: a modifiable risk factor for cesarean delivery?
A:
chosen: The study investigated whether knowledge of ultrasound-obtained estimated fetal weight (US-EFW) is a risk factor for cesarean delivery (CD). In a retrospective cohort of 2329 singleton, term live births from a single center in 2009-2010, 50.2% of women had US-EFW within 1 month of delivery. CD was significantly more common for women with US-EFW (15.7% vs 10.2%; P<.001). After controlling for confounders, US-EFW remained an independent risk factor for CD (odds ratio, 1.44; 95% confidence interval, 1.1-1.9). The risk increased when US-EFW was>3500 g (odds ratio, 1.8; 95% confidence interval, 1.3-2.7).
rejected:
Estimated fetal weight (EFW) by ultrasound is **not a modifiable risk factor** for cesarean delivery, as it reflects the baby's size rather than an adjustable condition. However, **incorrect EFW estimation** (due to technical or interpretation errors) or **management decisions** based on EFW (e.g., elective cesarean for suspected macrosomia) can influence cesarean rates. Thus, while EFW itself is non-modifiable, clinical actions taken in response to it may be adjusted to reduce unnecessary cesarean deliveries.  

**Key Point:** EFW is a **predictor**, not a modifiable factor, but its use in decision-making can be optimized to lower cesarean rates.
