To investigate the effectiveness of acupuncture in treating phonotraumatic vocal fold lesions.STUDY DESIGN/
A total of 123 dysphonic individuals with benign vocal pathologies were recruited. They were given either genuine acupuncture (n = 40), sham acupuncture (n = 44), or no treatment (n = 39) for 6 weeks (two 30-minute sessions/wk). The genuine acupuncture group received needles puncturing nine voice-related acupoints for 30 minutes, two times a week for 6 weeks, whereas the sham acupuncture group received blunted needles stimulating the skin surface of the nine acupoints for the same frequency and duration. The no-treatment group did not receive any intervention but attended just the assessment sessions. One-hundred seventeen subjects completed the study (genuine acupuncture = 40; sham acupuncture = 43; and no treatment = 34), but only 84 of them had a complete set of vocal functions and quality of life measures (genuine acupuncture = 29; sham acupuncture = 33; and no-treatment = 22) and 42 of them with a complete set of endoscopic data (genuine acupuncture = 16; sham acupuncture = 15; and no treatment = 11).
Significant improvement in vocal function, as indicated by the maximum fundamental frequency produced, and also perceived quality of life, were found in both the genuine and sham acupuncture groups, but not in the no-treatment group. Structural (morphological) improvements were, however, only noticed in the genuine acupuncture group, which demonstrated a significant reduction in the size of the vocal fold lesions.

Q: Is Acupuncture Efficacious for Treating Phonotraumatic Vocal Pathologies?
A:
chosen:
The study investigated the effectiveness of acupuncture in treating phonotraumatic vocal fold lesions by recruiting 123 dysphonic individuals with benign vocal pathologies. Participants were divided into genuine acupuncture (n = 40), sham acupuncture (n = 44), or no treatment (n = 39) groups for 6 weeks, with two 30-minute sessions per week. The genuine acupuncture group received needles puncturing nine voice-related acupoints for 30 minutes, two times a week for 6 weeks, whereas the sham acupuncture group received blunted needles stimulating the skin surface of the nine acupoints for the same frequency and duration. The no-treatment group did not receive any intervention but attended just the assessment sessions.  

Significant improvement in vocal function, as indicated by the maximum fundamental frequency produced, and also perceived quality of life, were found in both the genuine and sham acupuncture groups, but not in the no-treatment group. Structural (morphological) improvements were, however, only noticed in the genuine acupuncture group, which demonstrated a significant reduction in the size of the vocal fold lesions.  

The study included 117 completers (genuine acupuncture = 40; sham acupuncture = 43; and no treatment = 34), but only 84 of them had a complete set of vocal functions and quality of life measures (genuine acupuncture = 29; sham acupuncture = 33; and no treatment = 22) and 42 of them with a complete set of endoscopic data (genuine acupuncture = 16; sham acupuncture = 15; and no treatment = 11).  

Acupuncture shows efficacy in improving vocal function and structural outcomes for phonotraumatic vocal fold lesions.
rejected:
Current evidence suggests that **acupuncture may provide some benefits** for phonotraumatic vocal pathologies (e.g., vocal nodules, polyps, or muscle tension dysphonia), but results are mixed and limited by small sample sizes and methodological weaknesses in studies. Some trials report improvements in vocal function, reduced discomfort, and decreased reliance on conventional therapies, while others find no significant difference compared to sham acupuncture or standard care.  

**Conclusion:** Acupuncture may serve as a **complementary therapy**, but it should not replace evidence-based treatments like voice therapy or surgery when clinically indicated. More rigorous, large-scale studies are needed to confirm efficacy.

Irregular bleeding affects many users of combined menopausal hormone therapy (HT) and commonly leads to invasive and expensive investigations to exclude underlying malignancy. In most cases no abnormality is found.
The main objective of this study was to explore the role of uterine natural killer (uNK) cells and their regulatory cytokine IL-15 in irregular bleeding in HT users.
This was a prospective observational study conducted between 2002 and 2004.
The study was conducted in a tertiary referral menopause clinic at King Edward Memorial Hospital, Western Australia.
Patients included 117 postmenopausal women taking combined HT.
Outpatient endometrial biopsies were taken during and outside bleeding episodes.
The relationship between endometrial uNK cells (CD56+) and bleeding patterns was measured. We also addressed the impact of HT exposure on uNK cell populations, the relationship between endometrial IL-15 expression and uNK cell populations, and killer Ig like receptor genotype in subjects with irregular bleeding.
Endometrial CD56+ uNK cells were significantly increased in biopsies obtained during bleeding episodes (P<0.001), compared with HT users with no bleeding. The highest level of IL-15 expression was also seen in biopsies taken during bleeding. No clear relationship between killer Ig like receptor genotype and bleeding on HT was observed.

chosen:

The findings showed that endometrial CD56+ uNK cells were significantly increased in biopsies obtained during bleeding episodes compared with HT users with no bleeding (P<0.001). The highest level of IL-15 expression was also seen in biopsies taken during bleeding. No clear relationship between killer Ig like receptor genotype and bleeding on HT was observed.

No research had been undertaken to ascertain if this reflects the oral health of recruits joining each Service. It remains controversial whether there is a gender difference in survival of patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer.
We retrospectively analyzed 2770 patients (1689 men and 1081 women) with non-small cell lung cancer who underwent pulmonary resection between 1995 and 2005 at the National Cancer Center Hospital, Tokyo. A gender difference in survival was studied in all patients, in those divided according to histology or pathologic stage, and in propensity-matched gender pairs.
There were no differences in background, such as preoperative pulmonary function, operation procedures, or operative mortality. The proportions of adenocarcinoma and pathologic stage I in women were greater than those in men (93.6% vs 61.7% and 71.4% vs 58.6%, respectively) (P<.001). Overall 5-year survival of women was better than that of men (81% vs 70%, P<.001). In adenocarcinoma, the overall 5-year survival for women was better than that for men in pathologic stage I (95% vs 87%, P<.001) and in pathologic stage II or higher (58% vs 51%, P = .017). In non-adenocarcinoma, there was no significant gender difference in survival in pathologic stage I (P = .313) or pathologic stage II or higher (P = .770). The variables such as age, smoking status, histology, and pathologic stage were used for propensity score matching, and survival analysis of propensity score-matched gender pairs did not show a significant difference (P = .69).

Q: Gender difference in survival of resected non-small cell lung cancer: histology-related phenomenon?
A:
chosen: The study analyzed 2770 patients (1689 men and 1081 women) with resected non-small cell lung cancer, finding no differences in background factors like preoperative pulmonary function, operation procedures, or operative mortality. Women had higher proportions of adenocarcinoma (93.6% vs 61.7%) and pathologic stage I (71.4% vs 58.6%) compared to men. Overall 5-year survival was better for women (81% vs 70%). In adenocarcinoma, women had better survival in pathologic stage I (95% vs 87%) and stage II or higher (58% vs 51%). No significant gender difference in survival was observed for non-adenocarcinoma in stage I or stage II or higher. Propensity score-matched analysis using variables like age, smoking status, histology, and pathologic stage showed no significant survival difference between genders.

The technique of induced sputum has allowed to subdivide asthma patients into inflammatory phenotypes according to their level of granulocyte airway infiltration. There are very few studies which looked at detailed sputum and blood cell counts in a large cohort of asthmatics divided into inflammatory phenotypes. The purpose of this study was to analyze sputum cell counts, blood leukocytes and systemic inflammatory markers in these phenotypes, and investigate how those groups compared with healthy subjects.
We conducted a retrospective cross-sectional study on 833 asthmatics recruited from the University Asthma Clinic of Liege and compared them with 194 healthy subjects. Asthmatics were classified into inflammatory phenotypes.
The total non-squamous cell count per gram of sputum was greater in mixed granulocytic and neutrophilic phenotypes as compared to eosinophilic, paucigranulocytic asthma and healthy subjects (p < 0.005). Sputum eosinophils (in absolute values and percentages) were increased in all asthma phenotypes including paucigranulocytic asthma, compared to healthy subjects (p < 0.005). Eosinophilic asthma showed higher absolute sputum neutrophil and lymphocyte counts than healthy subjects (p < 0.005), while neutrophilic asthmatics had a particularly low number of sputum macrophages and epithelial cells. All asthma phenotypes showed an increased blood leukocyte count compared to healthy subjects (p < 0.005), with paucigranulocytic asthmatics having also increased absolute blood eosinophils compared to healthy subjects (p < 0.005). Neutrophilic asthma had raised CRP and fibrinogen while eosinophilic asthma only showed raised fibrinogen compared to healthy subjects (p < 0.005).

Q: Detailed analysis of sputum and systemic inflammation in asthma phenotypes: are paucigranulocytic asthmatics really non-inflammatory?
A:
chosen: The study analyzed sputum cell counts, blood leukocytes, and systemic inflammatory markers in 833 asthmatics compared to 194 healthy subjects, classifying asthmatics into inflammatory phenotypes. The total non-squamous cell count per gram of sputum was greater in mixed granulocytic and neutrophilic phenotypes than in eosinophilic, paucigranulocytic asthma, and healthy subjects (p < 0.005). Sputum eosinophils, in absolute values and percentages, were increased in all asthma phenotypes, including paucigranulocytic asthma, compared to healthy subjects (p < 0.005). Eosinophilic asthma showed higher absolute sputum neutrophil and lymphocyte counts than healthy subjects (p < 0.005), while neutrophilic asthmatics had particularly low numbers of sputum macrophages and epithelial cells. All asthma phenotypes had increased blood leukocyte counts compared to healthy subjects (p < 0.005), with paucigranulocytic asthmatics also showing increased absolute blood eosinophils (p < 0.005). Neutrophilic asthma had raised CRP and fibrinogen, while eosinophilic asthma only showed raised fibrinogen compared to healthy subjects (p < 0.005). These findings demonstrate that paucigranulocytic asthmatics exhibit elevated sputum eosinophils and blood leukocytes, indicating they are not truly non-inflammatory.
rejected: Paucigranulocytic asthma (PGA) is traditionally considered non-inflammatory due to the absence of significant eosinophilic or neutrophilic infiltration in sputum. However, emerging evidence suggests that PGA may still involve systemic and localized airway inflammation, albeit at lower levels or through different pathways (e.g., mast cell activation, epithelial dysfunction, or non-type 2 cytokines). Thus, while PGA appears less inflammatory compared to eosinophilic or neutrophilic phenotypes, it is not entirely non-inflammatory. Further research is needed to clarify underlying mechanisms.

Most staging systems for soft tissue sarcoma are based on histologic malignancy-grade, tumor size and tumor depth. These factors are generally dichotomized, size at 5 cm. We believe it is unlikely that tumor depth per se should influence a tumor's metastatic capability. Therefore we hypothesized that the unfavourable prognostic importance of depth could be explained by the close association between size and depth, deep-seated tumors on average being larger than the superficial ones. When tumor size is dichotomized, this effect should be most pronounced in the large size (>5 cm) group in which the size span is larger.
We analyzed the associations between tumor size and depth and the prognostic importance of grade, size and depth in a population-based series of 490 adult patients with soft tissue sarcoma of the extremity or trunk wall with complete, 4.5 years minimum, follow-up.
Multivariate analysis showed no major prognostic effect of tumor depth when grade and size were taken into account. The mean size of small tumors was the same whether superficial or deep but the mean size of large and deep-seated tumors were one third larger than that of large but superficial tumors. Tumor depth influenced the prognosis in the subset of high-grade and large tumors. In this subset deep-seated tumors had poorer survival rate than superficial tumors, which could be explained by the larger mean size of the deep-seated tumors.

Q: Should tumor depth be included in prognostication of soft tissue sarcoma?
A:
chosen:
Most staging systems for soft tissue sarcoma include tumor depth along with histologic malignancy-grade and tumor size, with factors generally dichotomized at 5 cm. However, tumor depth per se is unlikely to influence a tumor's metastatic capability. The unfavorable prognostic importance of depth may be explained by the close association between size and depth, as deep-seated tumors are on average larger than superficial ones. This effect is most pronounced in the large size (>5 cm) group where the size span is larger. 

Multivariate analysis of 490 adult patients with soft tissue sarcoma of the extremity or trunk wall showed no major prognostic effect of tumor depth when grade and size were accounted for. The mean size of small tumors was similar for superficial and deep tumors, but large and deep-seated tumors were one third larger than large superficial tumors. Tumor depth influenced prognosis only in the subset of high-grade and large tumors, where deep-seated tumors had poorer survival rates than superficial tumors, likely due to their larger mean size.
rejected: Yes, tumor depth (superficial vs. deep relative to the fascia) should be included in the prognostication of soft tissue sarcoma, as it is a well-established independent prognostic factor. Deep tumors are associated with a higher risk of local recurrence, metastasis, and worse survival compared to superficial tumors. Major staging systems (e.g., AJCC, TNM) incorporate depth to improve risk stratification.

To assess whether perspective-taking, which researchers in other fields have shown to induce empathy, improves patient satisfaction in encounters between student-clinicians and standardized patients (SPs).

Phagocytic NADPH oxidase activity and leptin levels were enhanced (P<0.05) in obese patients compared with controls. NADPH oxidase activity positively correlated with leptin in obese patients. This association remained significant in a multivariate analysis. cIMT was higher (P<0.05) in obese patients compared with controls. In addition, cIMT also correlated positively with leptin and NADPH oxidase activity in obese patients. In-vitro studies showed that leptin induced NADPH oxidase activation. Inhibition of the leptin-induced NADPH oxidase activity by wortmannin and bisindolyl maleimide suggested a direct involvement of the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase and protein kinase C pathways, respectively. Finally, leptin-induced NADPH oxidase activation promoted macrophage proliferation.

Q: Is leptin involved in phagocytic NADPH oxidase overactivity in obesity?
A:
chosen: Hyperleptinemia and oxidative stress play a major role in the development of cardiovascular diseases in obesity. Phagocytic NADPH oxidase activity and leptin levels were enhanced in obese patients compared with controls, and NADPH oxidase activity positively correlated with leptin in obese patients. This association remained significant in a multivariate analysis. In-vitro studies showed that leptin induced NADPH oxidase activation, and inhibition of the leptin-induced NADPH oxidase activity by wortmannin and bisindolyl maleimide suggested a direct involvement of the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase and protein kinase C pathways, respectively. rejected:
Yes, leptin is involved in phagocytic NADPH oxidase overactivity in obesity. Elevated leptin levels in obesity can stimulate NADPH oxidase (NOX2) in phagocytes, leading to increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, which contributes to oxidative stress and inflammation. Leptin enhances NOX2 activity through pathways involving JAK2/STAT3 and PI3K/Akt signaling. prompt:
Aromatase inhibitors (AIs) are an effective treatment for postmenopausal women with hormone receptor-positive breast cancer. However, patients receiving AIs report a higher incidence of musculoskeletal symptoms and bone fractures; the mechanism and risk factors for this correlation are not well studied. The aim of this study was to correlate these musculoskeletal symptoms and bone fractures in patients receiving AIs with bone mineral density (BMD), previous tamoxifen use, and administration of calcium/bisphosphonate (Ca/Bis).
We reviewed charts of 856 patients with hormone receptor-positive nonmetastatic breast cancer seen at our institution between January 1999 and October 2007. A total of 316 patients met the inclusion criteria of treatment with one of the AIs for>or = 3 months and availability of a dualenergy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) during this treatment. Arthralgia, generalized bone pain and/or myalgia, bone fracture after beginning AIs, any tamoxifen treatment, and Ca/Bis therapy were recorded.
Our study demonstrates a significant association between symptoms and DEXA-BMD results (P<.001). Similarly, the group receiving tamoxifen before AIs had fewer patients with arthralgia or generalized bone pain/myalgia or bone fracture (P<.001). Furthermore, the group receiving AIs plus Ca/Bis had more patients without musculoskeletal symptoms and had fewer fractures. Finally, the group receiving steroidal AIs compared with nonsteroidal AIs had more patients with arthralgia or generalized bone pain and/or myalgia, and bone fractures (P<.001).

Q: Aromatase inhibitor-related musculoskeletal symptoms: is preventing osteoporosis the key to eliminating these symptoms?
A:
chosen:
Aromatase inhibitors (AIs) are an effective treatment for postmenopausal women with hormone receptor-positive breast cancer, but patients receiving AIs report a higher incidence of musculoskeletal symptoms and bone fractures. The study aimed to correlate these symptoms and fractures with bone mineral density (BMD), previous tamoxifen use, and administration of calcium/bisphosphonate (Ca/Bis).  

The study reviewed 316 patients treated with AIs for ≥3 months who had a dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan during treatment. It recorded arthralgia, generalized bone pain and/or myalgia, bone fractures after beginning AIs, tamoxifen treatment, and Ca/Bis therapy.  

The results showed a significant association between musculoskeletal symptoms and DEXA-BMD results. Patients who received tamoxifen before AIs had fewer arthralgia, generalized bone pain/myalgia, or bone fractures. Those receiving AIs plus Ca/Bis had fewer musculoskeletal symptoms and fractures. Additionally, patients on steroidal AIs had more arthralgia, generalized bone pain/myalgia, and fractures compared to nonsteroidal AIs.
rejected: No, preventing osteoporosis is not the key to eliminating aromatase inhibitor (AI)-related musculoskeletal symptoms (AIMSS). While AIs increase bone loss and osteoporosis risk, AIMSS are thought to arise from other mechanisms, such as estrogen deprivation in joints, tendons, and muscles, or inflammatory pathways. Bisphosphonates or other bone-protective agents can reduce fracture risk but do not consistently alleviate AIMSS. Management of AIMSS typically involves exercise, physical therapy, pain relief, or switching to alternative hormonal therapies.

prompt:
To determine whether there are differences between blood pressure (BP) measured by the nurse (NBP), BP measured by the physician (PBP) and self-measured BP in treated hypertensive patients and, if found, to evaluate their clinical importance.
An observational study is carried out with hypertensive patients recruited from two village-based community health centres in Catalonia (Spain) serving an area with a total population of 2800 inhabitants. All patients treated for hypertension visiting the health centre on a specific day of the week and during the same timetable between October 2000 and May 2001 were included.
The difference between physician-systolic BP and nurse-systolic BP was 5.16 mmHg (95% CI 2.62-7.7; p<0.001). The difference between physician-systolic BP and self-measured systolic BP was 4.67 mmHg (95% CI 0.89-8.44; p=0.016). The differences between nurse-systolic BP and self-measured systolic BP were not significant (0.49 mmHg; 95% CI 3.71-2.71; p=0.758). With regards to diastolic BP, no significant differences were found between the different ways of measurement. NBP gave the following values: sensitivity (Sn) of 92% and specificity (Sp) of 60%; positive predictive value (PPV) of 65.7% and negative predictive value (NPV) of 90% with a positive coefficient of probability (CP+) of 2.3 and a negative coefficient of probability (CP-) of 0.133. PBP gave the following results: Sn=72%; Sp=66.7%; PPV=64.3%; NPV=74.1%; CP+=2.16 and CP- = 0.420.

Q: Does blood pressure change in treated hypertensive patients depending on whether it is measured by a physician or a nurse?
A:
chosen:
In treated hypertensive patients recruited from two village-based community health centres in Catalonia (Spain) between October 2000 and May 2001, systolic blood pressure differed significantly depending on whether it was measured by a physician or a nurse. The difference between physician-systolic BP and nurse-systolic BP was 5.16 mmHg (95% CI 2.62-7.7; p<0.001).  

The difference between physician-systolic BP and self-measured systolic BP was 4.67 mmHg (95% CI 0.89-8.44; p=0.016), while the differences between nurse-systolic BP and self-measured systolic BP were not significant (0.49 mmHg; 95% CI 3.71-2.71; p=0.758). No significant differences were found in diastolic BP between the measurement methods.  

The study included hypertensive patients from two village-based community health centres serving an area with a total population of 2800 inhabitants. All patients treated for hypertension visiting the health centre on a specific day of the week and during the same timetable between October 2000 and May 2001 were included.
rejected:
Yes, blood pressure measurements can differ between physicians and nurses in treated hypertensive patients, a phenomenon partly attributed to the **"white-coat effect."** Studies suggest that patients may exhibit slightly higher readings when measured by physicians compared to nurses, likely due to heightened anxiety or subconscious stress responses. However, the difference is often modest (typically <5 mmHg) and may vary by clinical context. Automated office blood pressure (AOBP) or out-of-office measurements (e.g., home monitoring) can help mitigate this variability.  

*Key point:* While the disparity exists, its clinical significance depends on the magnitude and consistency of the difference in individual patients.

prompt:
We explored whether QT corrected dispersion (QTcD) can identify left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) in hypertensives.
We enrolled 100 hypertensive patients (study group) and 30 normotensive subjects (control group). Echocardiography was performed to measure left ventricular mass and left ventricular mass index. Electrocardiogram was performed to measure QTcD.
LVH was present in 42 patients (42%) of the study group, none among controls. Hypertensive patients had significantly greater indices of LVH and QTcD compared with controls (p<0.001 for all). Similarly, among hypertensive patients, those with LVH had a significantly greater QTcD compared with those without (p<0.001). Pearson's correlation coefficient test demonstrated strongly positive correlations between QTcD and the indices of LVH (p<0.001 for all). Analysis of the receiver operating characteristic curves identified 60 ms as the optimal cut-off value of QTcD that best predicts LVH in hypertensives. Using this value, QTcD was able to predict LVH with a sensitivity of 92.9% and specificity 98.2%.

Q: Would corrected QT dispersion predict left ventricular hypertrophy in hypertensive patients?
A:
chosen: The study explored whether corrected QT dispersion (QTcD) can identify left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) in hypertensive patients. Among 100 hypertensive patients, LVH was present in 42 patients (42%), and none in the 30 normotensive controls. Hypertensive patients had significantly greater indices of LVH and QTcD compared with controls (p<0.001 for all). Hypertensive patients with LVH had a significantly greater QTcD than those without LVH (p<0.001). Pearson's correlation coefficient test showed strongly positive correlations between QTcD and LVH indices (p<0.001 for all). The receiver operating characteristic curve analysis identified 60 ms as the optimal QTcD cut-off value to predict LVH in hypertensives, with a sensitivity of 92.9% and specificity of 98.2%.
rejected:
Current evidence suggests that **corrected QT dispersion (QTcd) is not a reliable or consistent predictor of left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) in hypertensive patients**. While some studies have reported weak associations between increased QTcd and LVH, others found no significant correlation. QTcd lacks sufficient sensitivity and specificity for this purpose, and **echocardiography or cardiac MRI remain the gold standards** for diagnosing LVH in hypertension.  

Thus, QTcd is **not recommended as a standalone predictor** of LVH in clinical practice.

prompt:
To determine the association between fetal biometry in the first or early second trimester and severe macrosomia at delivery.
This case-control study included 30 term severely macrosomic neonates; 90 appropriate-for-gestational age (AGA) neonates served as controls. All pregnancies underwent nuchal translucency (NT) screening at 11-14 weeks' gestation. Pregnancies were dated by accurate last menstrual period consistent with crown-rump length (CRL) measurements at the time of screening, early pregnancy CRL or date of fertilization. The association between birth weight and the difference between the measured and the expected CRL at the time of NT screening was analyzed.
The difference between measured and expected CRL, expressed both in mm and in days of gestation, was statistically greater in the severely macrosomic neonates compared with controls (mean, 6.66 +/- 4.78 mm vs. 1.17 +/- 4.6 mm, P<0.0001 and 3 +/- 2.2 days vs. 0.5 +/- 2.3 days, P<0.0001, respectively). Furthermore, there were significant correlations between the extent of macrosomia and the discrepancy between expected and measured fetal size at the time of NT screening (r = 0.47, P<0.01 and r = 0.48, P<0.01, respectively).

Q: Is severe macrosomia manifested at 11-14 weeks of gestation?
A:
chosen: The study found that severe macrosomia at delivery was associated with a statistically greater difference between measured and expected crown-rump length (CRL) at 11-14 weeks' gestation compared to controls. The mean difference was 6.66 +/- 4.78 mm vs. 1.17 +/- 4.6 mm (P<0.0001) and 3 +/- 2.2 days vs. 0.5 +/- 2.3 days (P<0.0001) for severely macrosomic neonates and appropriate-for-gestational age neonates, respectively. Significant correlations were observed between the extent of macrosomia and the discrepancy between expected and measured fetal size at the time of NT screening (r = 0.47, P<0.01 and r = 0.48, P<0.01).
rejected: No, severe macrosomia (excessive fetal growth) is not typically manifested at 11-14 weeks of gestation. Macrosomia is usually diagnosed later in pregnancy, often in the third trimester, as fetal growth accelerates. Early pregnancy ultrasounds (11-14 weeks) primarily assess fetal viability, dating, and early anatomy, not macrosomia. Risk factors (e.g., maternal diabetes) may be identified early, but the condition itself becomes apparent later.

prompt:
Peripheral venous thrombophlebitis (PVT) is a common complication of intravenous cannulation, occurring in about 30% of patients. We evaluated the effect of elective re-siting of intravenous cannulae every 48 hours on the incidence and severity of PVT in patients receiving intravenous fluids/drugs.
We randomized 42 patients who were admitted for major abdominal surgery to either the control or study group (n = 21 in either group). Informed consent was obtained from all of them. Cannulae in the control group were removed only if the site became painful, the cannula got dislodged or there were signs and symptoms suggestive of PVT, namely pain, erythema, swelling, excessive warmth or a palpable venous cord. Cannulae in the study group were changed and re-sited electively every 48 hours. All the patients were examined every 24 hours for signs and symptoms of PVT at the current and previous sites of infusion.
The incidence of PVT was 100% (21/21) in the control group and only 9.5% (2/21) in the study group (p<0.0001). The severity of PVT was also less in the study group compared with that in the control group. Day-wise correlation of the incidence of PVT showed that 82.6% of the episodes of PVT occurred on day 3.

Q: Does elective re-siting of intravenous cannulae decrease peripheral thrombophlebitis?
A:
chosen: The study evaluated the effect of elective re-siting of intravenous cannulae every 48 hours on the incidence and severity of peripheral venous thrombophlebitis (PVT) in patients receiving intravenous fluids/drugs. The control group had cannulae removed only if the site became painful, the cannula got dislodged, or there were signs of PVT (pain, erythema, swelling, excessive warmth, or a palpable venous cord). The study group had cannulae changed and re-sited electively every 48 hours. The incidence of PVT was 100% (21/21) in the control group and only 9.5% (2/21) in the study group (p<0.0001). The severity of PVT was also less in the study group. Day-wise correlation showed that 82.6% of PVT episodes occurred on day 3.
rejected: Yes, elective re-siting of intravenous cannulae (typically every 72–96 hours) has been shown to decrease the incidence of peripheral thrombophlebitis. However, the evidence is mixed, and some studies suggest that routine replacement may not significantly reduce complications compared to clinically indicated replacement. Current guidelines often recommend assessing cannulas daily and removing or re-siting them only if signs of inflammation, dysfunction, or infection are present.

prompt:
Little is known about whether a routinely inserted endoscopic nasobiliary drainage (ENBD) tube improves the clinical course in patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis after clearance of choledocholithiasis.
The aim of this study was to investigate the need for ENBD on the clinical outcomes of patients with acute cholangitis undergoing endoscopic clearance of common bile duct (CBD) stones.
Prospective, randomized study.
Tertiary referral center.
A total of 104 patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis who underwent primary endoscopic treatment were compared according to insertion of an ENBD tube (51 in the ENBD group and 53 in the no-ENBD group).
Insertion of an ENBD tube after clearance of CBD stones.
Recurrence of cholangitis and length of hospital stay after clearance of CBD stones.
Baseline clinical characteristics were similar between both groups. There were no significant differences in the recurrence rate of cholangitis at 24 weeks (3.9% for the ENBD group vs 3.8% for the no-ENBD group at 24 weeks; P = .99) and length of hospital stay (7.9 days [standard error = 1.2] for the ENBD group vs 7.9 days [standard error = 0.7]for the no-ENBD group; P = .98). However, procedure time was longer (26.2 [SE = 1.8] minutes vs 22.7 [SE = 1.0]minutes, respectively; P = .01) and the discomfort score was higher (4.9 [SE = 0.4] vs 2.8 [SE = 0.3], respectively; P = .02) in the ENBD group than in the no-ENBD group.
Single-center study.

Q: Is it necessary to insert a nasobiliary drainage tube routinely after endoscopic clearance of the common bile duct in patients with choledocholithiasis-induced cholangitis?
A:
chosen: The study found no significant differences in the recurrence rate of cholangitis at 24 weeks (3.9% for the ENBD group vs 3.8% for the no-ENBD group) or in the length of hospital stay (7.9 days for both groups) between patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis who received an ENBD tube after endoscopic clearance of CBD stones and those who did not. However, the ENBD group had a longer procedure time (26.2 minutes vs 22.7 minutes) and a higher discomfort score (4.9 vs 2.8) compared to the no-ENBD group. The baseline clinical characteristics were similar between both groups. The study was a single-center, prospective, randomized trial involving 104 patients.
rejected:
Routine insertion of a **nasobiliary drainage (NBD) tube** after endoscopic clearance of the common bile duct (CBD) in **choledocholithiasis-induced cholangitis** is **not necessary** in most cases.  

Current evidence suggests that **endoscopic biliary drainage (via stent or NBD) is beneficial in severe or unresolved cholangitis**, but **NBD is not superior to plastic stenting** and is less comfortable for patients. A **plastic biliary stent** is often preferred due to similar efficacy and better patient tolerance.  

**Exceptions** where NBD may be considered include:  
- Need for repeated cholangiography (e.g., incomplete stone clearance).  
- Severe purulent cholangitis requiring frequent irrigation.  
- High suspicion of residual stones or need for post-procedure monitoring.  

**Conclusion:** Routine NBD is not mandatory if complete stone clearance is achieved and adequate biliary drainage is confirmed. A stent may be sufficient in most cases.  

*(Sources: Tokyo Guidelines 2018, ESGE guidelines, and clinical studies.)*

prompt:
Childhood obesity is pandemic condition. The effect of obesity on trauma outcomes in children has been relatively understudied. We conducted this study to ascertain the effects of obesity on the hospital outcome of injured children.
A retrospective cohort study of patients aged 2 to 18 years admitted to the King Abdul Aziz Medical City between May 2001 and May 2009 was conducted. Patients were categorized as lean (body mass index<95th percentile) and obese (body mass index ≥ 95th percentile). Groups were compared regarding admission demographics, mechanism of injury, pattern of injury, length of stay, intensive care unit admission, ventilation duration, types of procedures performed, injury severity score, and mortality.
Nine hundred thirty-three patients were included, of those 55 (5.89%) children were obese. The obese children were older than nonobese (P = .001) and had a higher injury severity score (P = .001) and a lower pediatric trauma score (P = .00), heart rate (P = .0081), and respiratory rate (P = .000). There were no differences between groups with regard to sex, mechanism of injury, and surgical procedures. Obese children were more likely to have rib fractures (P = .02) and pelvic injuries (P = .033). There was no significant association between mortality and obesity (P = .42).

Q: Does obesity impact the pattern and outcome of trauma in children?
A:
chosen: The study included 933 patients aged 2 to 18 years admitted to King Abdul Aziz Medical City between May 2001 and May 2009, of whom 55 (5.89%) were obese (body mass index ≥ 95th percentile). Obese children were older than nonobese children (P = .001) and had a higher injury severity score (P = .001), a lower pediatric trauma score (P = .00), lower heart rate (P = .0081), and lower respiratory rate (P = .000). There were no differences in sex, mechanism of injury, or surgical procedures between the groups. Obese children were more likely to have rib fractures (P = .02) and pelvic injuries (P = .033). No significant association was found between mortality and obesity (P = .42). Obesity impacted trauma outcomes through higher injury severity scores and specific injuries like rib and pelvic fractures, but not mortality.
rejected: Yes, obesity can impact the pattern and outcome of trauma in children. They also face worse outcomes, including longer hospital stays, higher complication rates (e.g., infections, respiratory issues), and greater difficulty in diagnosis and treatment. Additionally, obesity may increase the severity of trauma due to higher energy impacts and associated metabolic disorders.

Identifying eating behaviors which contribute to excess weight gain will inform obesity prevention strategies. A tendency to clear one's plate when eating may be a risk factor for obesity in an environment where food is plentiful. Whether plate clearing is associated with increased body weight in a cohort of US participants was examined.
Nine hundred and ninety-three US adults (60% male, 80% American European, mean age=31 years) completed self-report measures of habitual plate clearing together with behavioral and demographic characteristics known to be associated with obesity.
Plate clearing tendencies were positively associated with BMI and remained so after accounting for a large number of other demographic and behavioral predictors of BMI in analyses (β=0.18, 95% CIs=0.07, 0.29, P<0.001); an increased tendency to plate clear was associated with a significantly higher body weight.

rejected:

prompt:
A higher prevalence of cardiovascular risk factors (CRFs) in HIV-infected patients, together with chronic infection and treatments, has resulted in an increased risk of silent myocardial ischaemia (SMI). The objective of this study was to evaluate whether myocardial SPECT should be used for screening HIV-infected patients with no clinical symptoms of coronary artery disease.
The prevalence of SMI detected by myocardial SPECT was determined in 94 HIV-infected patients with a normal clinical cardiovascular examination in relation to anthropomorphic parameters, CRFs, inflammatory and HIV infection status, and treatment.
Coronary artery disease was detected in nine patients (eight with ischaemia, one with myocardial infarction), corresponding to 9.6 % positivity. All but two of the scintigraphic diagnoses of ischaemia were confirmed by coronarography. Univariate analysis revealed that the overall number of CRFs and the combination of gender and age were associated with a diagnosis of SMI (p<0.05). According to multivariate analysis, the only independent parameter significantly associated with the scintigraphic diagnosis of SMI was the combination of gender and age (p = 0.01). All the positive myocardial SPECT scans were in men older than 52 years with at least two other CRFs. In this subpopulation of 47 patients, the prevalence of SMI detected by myocardial SPECT reached 19.2 %.

Q: Should HIV-infected patients be screened for silent myocardial ischaemia using gated myocardial perfusion SPECT?
A:
chosen: The prevalence of silent myocardial ischaemia (SMI) detected by myocardial SPECT in HIV-infected patients with no clinical symptoms of coronary artery disease was 9.6%, with coronary artery disease identified in nine patients (eight with ischaemia, one with myocardial infarction). Univariate analysis showed that the overall number of cardiovascular risk factors (CRFs) and the combination of gender and age were associated with SMI diagnosis. Multivariate analysis confirmed that the combination of gender and age was the only independent parameter significantly linked to SMI, with all positive SPECT scans occurring in men older than 52 years with at least two other CRFs. In this subpopulation of 47 patients, the prevalence of SMI reached 19.2%. These findings suggest that myocardial SPECT screening may be particularly relevant for older HIV-infected men with multiple CRFs.
rejected:
There is currently **no strong evidence** to support routine screening for silent myocardial ischemia using gated myocardial perfusion SPECT (MPS) in all HIV-infected patients. However, selective screening may be considered in **high-risk individuals** (e.g., those with traditional cardiovascular risk factors, long-standing HIV, or prior ART-related metabolic complications).  

The **2018 American Heart Association (AHA) statement on HIV and cardiovascular disease** suggests individualized risk assessment rather than universal screening. Gated MPS may be useful in symptomatic patients or those with high coronary artery disease (CAD) risk, but its role in asymptomatic HIV patients remains unclear.  

**Conclusion:** Routine screening is not recommended, but targeted use in high-risk patients may be justified. Always consider clinical context and risk factors.

To determine whether prior exposure of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs increases perioperative blood loss associated with major orthopaedic surgery.
Fifty patients scheduled for total hip replacement were allocated to two groups (double blind, randomized manner). rejected:

Previous studies reported that breast-feeding protects children against a variety of diseases, but these studies were generally conducted on "high-risk" or hospitalized children. This paper describes the results of our study on the effects of breast-feeding on rate of illness in normal children with a family history of atopy.
A historic cohort approach of 794 children with a family history of atopy was used to assess the effects of breast-feeding on illness rates. Family history of atopy was based on allergic diseases in family members as registered by the family physician. In the first year of life they had fewer episodes of gastroenteritis, lower respiratory tract infections, and digestive tract disorders. Over the next three years of life they had fewer respiratory tract infections and skin infections.

Q: Is the breast best for children with a family history of atopy?

chosen:

Breast-feeding was related to lower levels of childhood illness both in the first and the first three years of life. In the first year of life, breast-fed children had fewer episodes of gastroenteritis, lower respiratory tract infections, and digestive tract disorders. Incontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD) is a potentially serious skin injury that can lead to pressure ulcers (PUs). Multiple studies have indicated the need for evidence to find the most effective skin care protocol to reduce the incidence and severity of IAD in critically ill patients.
To compare the incidence and severity of IAD in two groups on a progressive care unit (PCU) using a defined skin care protocol: cleaning with a gentle cleanser and moisturizer, then applying a skin protectant/barrier. The control group received the skin care protocol every 12 hours and the interventional group received the protocol every 6 hours; both groups also received it as needed.
A 9-month randomized prospective study was conducted on 99 patients (N = 55 in the intervention group and N = 44 in the control group) who were incontinent of urine, stool, or both, or had a fecal diversion device or urinary catheter for more than 2 days.
The dermatitis score in the intervention group on discharge was significantly less (7.1%; P ≤ 0.001) in the moderate IAD group than in the control group (10.9%). The dermatitis score means and P values of each group were compared using a paired t test.

Q: Does skin care frequency affect the severity of incontinence-associated dermatitis in critically ill patients?
A:
chosen: The study compared the incidence and severity of incontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD) in critically ill patients using a defined skin care protocol applied every 6 hours (intervention group) versus every 12 hours (control group). The intervention group showed a significantly lower dermatitis score on discharge (7.1%) compared to the control group (10.9%), with a P value of ≤ 0.001, indicating that more frequent skin care reduced the severity of IAD. Both groups received the protocol as needed, which included cleaning with a gentle cleanser and moisturizer, followed by a skin protectant/barrier. The results were based on a 9-month randomized prospective study of 99 patients who were incontinent of urine, stool, or both, or had a fecal diversion device or urinary catheter for more than 2 days. The dermatitis score means and P values were compared using a paired t test.
rejected:
Yes, more frequent skin care (e.g., gentle cleansing, moisturizing, and barrier protection) can reduce the severity of **incontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD)** in critically ill patients by maintaining skin integrity and minimizing prolonged exposure to irritants like urine and feces. Studies suggest that **proactive, structured skin care protocols** (e.g., timely cleansing and barrier creams) help prevent or mitigate IAD severity in high-risk populations. However, excessive or harsh cleaning may worsen skin damage, so a balanced approach is key.  

*Key takeaway:* **Regular, gentle skin care reduces IAD severity, but frequency must be paired with proper techniques and products.**

prompt:
Patients living in rural areas may be at a disadvantage in accessing tertiary health care.AIM: To test the hypothesis that very premature infants born to mothers residing in rural areas have poorer outcomes than those residing in urban areas in the state of New South Wales (NSW) and the Australian Capital Territory (ACT) despite a coordinated referral and transport system.
"Rural" or "urban" status was based on the location of maternal residence. Perinatal characteristics, major morbidity and case mix adjusted mortality were compared between 1879 rural and 6775 urban infants<32 weeks gestational age, born in 1992-2002 and admitted to all 10 neonatal intensive care units in NSW and ACT.
Rural mothers were more likely to be teenaged, indigenous, and to have had a previous premature birth, prolonged ruptured membrane, and antenatal corticosteroid. Urban mothers were more likely to have had assisted conception and a caesarean section. More urban (93% v 83%) infants were born in a tertiary obstetric hospital. Infants of rural residence had a higher mortality (adjusted odds ratio (OR) 1.26, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.07 to 1.48, p = 0.005). This trend was consistently seen in all subgroups and significantly for the tertiary hospital born population and the 30-31 weeks gestation subgroup. Regional birth data in this gestational age range also showed a higher stillbirth rate among rural infants (OR 1.20, 95% CI 1.09 to 1.32, p<0.001).

Q: Does rural or urban residence make a difference to neonatal outcome in premature birth?
A:
chosen: Very premature infants born to mothers residing in rural areas in New South Wales and the Australian Capital Territory had poorer outcomes compared to those from urban areas, despite a coordinated referral and transport system. Rural mothers were more likely to be teenaged, indigenous, and to have had a previous premature birth, prolonged ruptured membrane, and antenatal corticosteroid, while urban mothers were more likely to have had assisted conception and a caesarean section. More urban infants (93% v 83%) were born in a tertiary obstetric hospital. Infants of rural residence had a higher mortality, with an adjusted odds ratio of 1.26. This trend was consistently seen in all subgroups, significantly for the tertiary hospital-born population and the 30-31 weeks gestation subgroup. Regional birth data also showed a higher stillbirth rate among rural infants, with an odds ratio of 1.20.
rejected:
Yes, rural residence is generally associated with worse neonatal outcomes in premature birth compared to urban areas. This is due to factors such as limited access to specialized neonatal care (e.g., NICUs), longer travel times to hospitals, and fewer healthcare resources. Urban centers typically have better-equipped facilities and quicker access to critical interventions, improving survival and reducing complications for preterm infants. However, outcomes can vary based on regional healthcare infrastructure and policies.

prompt:
It was the aim of the present study to elaborate criteria for the assessment of rapid hemodynamic progression of valvar aortic stenosis. These criteria are of special importance when cardiac surgery is indicated for other reasons but the established criteria for aortic valve replacement are not yet fulfilled. Such aspects of therapeutic planing were mostly disregarded in the past so that patients had to undergo cardiac reoperation within a few years.
Hemodynamic, echocardiographic, and clinical data of 169 men and 88 women with aortic stenosis, aged 55.2 +/- 15.7 years at their first and 63.4 +/- 15.6 years at their second cardiac catheterization, were analyzed.
The progression rate of aortic valve obstruction was found to be dependent on the degree of valvar calcification ([VC] scoring 0 to III) and to be exponentially correlated with the aortic valve opening area (AVA) at initial catheterization. Neither age nor sex of the patient nor etiology of the valvar obstruction significantly influence the progression of aortic stenosis. If AVA decreases below 0.75 cm(2) with a present degree of VC = 0, or AVA of 0.8 with VC of I, AVA of 0.9 with VC of II, or AVA of 1.0 with VC of III, it is probable that aortic stenosis will have to be operated upon in the following years.

Q: Can progression of valvar aortic stenosis be predicted accurately?
A:
chosen: The progression of valvar aortic stenosis can be predicted based on specific criteria. The progression rate depends on the degree of valvar calcification (scoring 0 to III) and is exponentially correlated with the aortic valve opening area (AVA) at initial catheterization. Neither age, sex, nor the etiology of the valvar obstruction significantly influences the progression. If AVA decreases below 0.75 cm² with VC = 0, AVA of 0.8 with VC of I, AVA of 0.9 with VC of II, or AVA of 1.0 with VC of III, it is probable that aortic stenosis will require surgery in the following years. These criteria are important for therapeutic planning, especially when cardiac surgery is indicated for other reasons but established criteria for aortic valve replacement are not yet fulfilled.
rejected: The progression of **valvular aortic stenosis (AS)** can be **estimated but not predicted with high accuracy** in individual patients. While certain risk factors (e.g., age, baseline valve calcification, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease) are associated with faster progression, the rate varies significantly among individuals. Serial echocardiographic monitoring remains the standard for tracking disease progression. Advanced imaging (e.g., CT for calcium scoring) and biomarkers may improve risk stratification, but precise prediction remains challenging.

To ascertain whether hospital type is associated with differences in total cost and outcomes for inpatient tonsillectomy.
Cross-sectional analysis of the 2006, 2009, and 2012 Kids' Inpatient Database (KID).
Children ≤18 years of age undergoing tonsillectomy with/without adenoidectomy were included. Risk-adjusted generalized linear models assessed for differences in hospital cost and length of stay (LOS) among children managed by (1) non-children's teaching hospitals (NCTHs), (2) children's teaching hospitals (CTHs), and (3) nonteaching hospitals (NTHs). Risk-adjusted logistic regression compared the odds of major perioperative complications (hemorrhage, respiratory failure, death). Models accounted for clustering of patients within hospitals, were weighted to provide national estimates, and controlled for comorbidities.
The 25,685 tonsillectomies recorded in the KID yielded a national estimate of 40,591 inpatient tonsillectomies performed in 2006, 2009, and 2012. The CTHs had significantly higher risk-adjusted total cost and LOS per tonsillectomy compared with NCTHs and NTHs ($9423.34/2.8 days, $6250.78/2.11 days, and $5905.10/2.08 days, respectively; P<.001). The CTHs had higher odds of complications compared with NCTHs (odds ratio [OR], 1.48; 95% CI, 1.15-1.91; P = .002) but not when compared with NTHs (OR, 1.19; 95% CI, 0.89-1.59; P = .23). The CTHs were significantly more likely to care for patients with comorbidities (P<.001).

Q: Inpatient Pediatric Tonsillectomy: Does Hospital Type Affect Cost and Outcomes of Care?
A:
chosen: The study analyzed 25,685 tonsillectomies from the 2006, 2009, and 2012 Kids' Inpatient Database (KID), yielding a national estimate of 40,591 inpatient tonsillectomies. Children’s teaching hospitals (CTHs) had significantly higher risk-adjusted total cost and length of stay (LOS) per tonsillectomy compared with non-children’s teaching hospitals (NCTHs) and nonteaching hospitals (NTHs), with costs of $9423.34/2.8 days for CTHs, $6250.78/2.11 days for NCTHs, and $5905.10/2.08 days for NTHs (P<.001). CTHs also had higher odds of major perioperative complications compared with NCTHs (odds ratio [OR], 1.48; 95% CI, 1.15-1.91; P = .002) but not when compared with NTHs (OR, 1.19; 95% CI, 0.89-1.59; P = .23). CTHs were significantly more likely to care for patients with comorbidities (P<.001).
rejected:
The type of hospital (e.g., children’s hospitals vs. general hospitals) can influence the cost and outcomes of pediatric tonsillectomy. Studies suggest that **children’s hospitals** often have **higher costs** due to specialized care but may offer **better outcomes** (e.g., lower complication rates, readmissions) compared to general or community hospitals. However, uncomplicated cases may have similar outcomes across settings, making cost-effectiveness context-dependent.  

*(Answer based on existing research trends; specific findings may vary by study.)*

prompt:
Associations between several psychopathological alterations and lowered beta-endorphin(beta E) plasma levels have already been stated in former studies. However, whereas single measures during static conditions generally failed in linking beta E levels with psychopathology, dynamic changes of beta E in particular have been shown to be associated with spells of anxiety and depression. During alcohol withdrawal, a decreased secretion of beta E with a delayed normalization has been reported, but up to now only few data became available regarding the interaction of plasma beta E and psychopathological parameters.
The aim of our study was to test the hypothesis whether beta E during acute alcohol withdrawal is associated with anxiety, depression, and craving.
We observed self-rated anxiety, depression, and craving during alcohol withdrawal and assessed beta E levels (RIA) in a consecutive sample of 60 alcoholics on day 1 and day 14 after onset of withdrawal, and in 30 healthy volunteers. To control for mutual interactions of beta E and the pituitary-adrenocortical hormone secretion, plasma corticotropin (ACTH) and cortisol were also determined.
In accordance with prior studies, beta E was significantly lowered on day 1 and day 14 of alcohol withdrawal relative to controls. Plasma levels of ACTH correlated significantly with beta E in alcoholics at both time points and in controls, without differing significantly between the groups. Self-rated anxiety, depression, and alcohol craving decreased significantly between day 1 and day 14. Levels of beta E were inversely correlated with anxiety day 1 (r=-0.58) and day 14 (r=-0.71). Partial correlation coefficients controlling for ACTH plasma levels revealed that this correlation was largely independent from ACTH. In addition, a significant inverse relationship was found between beta E and craving on day 14 (r=-0.28). No association appeared between beta E and depression.

Q: Is withdrawal-induced anxiety in alcoholism based on beta-endorphin deficiency?
A:
chosen: The study aimed to test the hypothesis that beta-endorphin (beta E) during acute alcohol withdrawal is associated with anxiety, depression, and craving. In a sample of 60 alcoholics and 30 healthy volunteers, beta E levels were significantly lowered on day 1 and day 14 of alcohol withdrawal relative to controls. Levels of beta E were inversely correlated with anxiety on day 1 (r=-0.58) and day 14 (r=-0.71), and partial correlation coefficients controlling for ACTH plasma levels showed this correlation was largely independent of ACTH. A significant inverse relationship was also found between beta E and craving on day 14 (r=-0.28). No association appeared between beta E and depression. These findings support the hypothesis that withdrawal-induced anxiety in alcoholism is linked to beta E deficiency.
rejected: Withdrawal-induced anxiety in alcoholism is partially linked to **beta-endorphin deficiency**, but it is not the sole mechanism. Chronic alcohol use disrupts the endogenous opioid system, reducing beta-endorphin levels, which contributes to dysphoria and anxiety during withdrawal. However, other factors—such as GABAergic inhibition, glutamatergic hyperactivity, and noradrenergic overactivity—also play significant roles. Thus, while beta-endorphin deficiency is involved, it is one component of a broader neurochemical imbalance.

To determine the impact of a physician's specialty on the frequency and depth of medication history documented in patient medical records.
A cross-sectional assessment of the frequency and depth of medication history information documented by 123 physicians for 900 randomly selected patients stratified across Cardiology, Chest, Dermatology, Endocrine, Gastroenterology, Haematology, Neurology, Psychiatry and Renal specialties was carried out at a 900-bed teaching hospital located in Ibadan, Nigeria.
Four hundred and forty-three (49.2%) of the cohort were males and 457 (50.8%) were females; with mean ages 43.2 +/- 18.6 and 43.1 +/- 17.9 years respectively. Physicians' specialties significantly influenced the depth of documentation of the medication history information across the nine specialties (P<0.0001). Post hoc pair-wise comparisons with Tukey's HSD test showed that the mean scores for adverse drug reactions and adherence to medicines was highest in the Cardiology specialty; while the Chest specialty had the highest mean scores for allergy to drugs, food, chemicals and cigarette smoking. Mean scores for the use of alcohol; illicit drugs; dietary restrictions was highest for Gastroenterology, Psychiatry and Endocrine specialties respectively. Physicians' specialties also significantly influenced the frequency of documentation of the medication history across the nine specialties (P<0.0001).

Q: Does a physician's specialty influence the recording of medication history in patients' case notes?
A:
chosen: The study assessed the impact of physician specialty on the frequency and depth of medication history documentation in patient medical records across nine specialties: Cardiology, Chest, Dermatology, Endocrine, Gastroenterology, Haematology, Neurology, Psychiatry, and Renal. Physicians' specialties significantly influenced both the depth (P<0.0001) and frequency (P<0.0001) of medication history documentation. Post hoc pair-wise comparisons with Tukey's HSD test revealed that Cardiology had the highest mean scores for adverse drug reactions and adherence to medicines, while the Chest specialty had the highest mean scores for allergy to drugs, food, chemicals, and cigarette smoking. Gastroenterology, Psychiatry, and Endocrine specialties had the highest mean scores for the use of alcohol, illicit drugs, and dietary restrictions, respectively. The study involved 123 physicians and 900 patients at a 900-bed teaching hospital in Ibadan, Nigeria.
rejected: Yes, a physician's specialty can influence the recording of medication history in patients' case notes. Studies suggest that specialists (e.g., cardiologists, geriatricians) may document medication histories more thoroughly than general practitioners, particularly for drugs relevant to their field. Factors like time constraints, clinical focus, and perceived relevance of medications to the specialty can affect documentation accuracy and completeness.

Tuberculosis has increased in parallel with the acquired immunodeficiency syndrome epidemic and the use of immunosuppressive therapy, and the growing incidence of extra-pulmonary tuberculosis, especially with intestinal involvement, reflects this trend. However, the duration of anti-tuberculous therapy has not been clarified in intestinal tuberculosis.AIM: To compare the efficacy of different treatment durations in tuberculous enterocolitis in terms of response and recurrence rates.
Forty patients with tuberculous enterocolitis were randomized prospectively: 22 patients into a 9-month and 18 into a 15-month group. Diagnosis was made either by colonoscopic findings of discrete ulcers and histopathological findings of caseating granuloma and/or acid-fast bacilli, or by clinical improvement after therapeutic trial. Patients were followed up with colonoscopy every other month until complete response or treatment completion, and then every 6 months for 1 year and annually. Complete response was defined as a resolution of symptoms and active tuberculosis by colonoscopy.
Complete response was obtained in all patients in both groups. Two patients in the 9-month group and one in the 15-month group underwent operation due to intestinal obstruction and perianal fistula, respectively. No recurrence of active intestinal tuberculosis occurred during the follow-up period in either group.

Q: Is a 9-month treatment sufficient in tuberculous enterocolitis?
A:
chosen: The study compared 9-month and 15-month anti-tuberculous therapy durations in 40 patients with tuberculous enterocolitis. Complete response was obtained in all patients in both groups. Two patients in the 9-month group and one in the 15-month group required surgery for complications (intestinal obstruction and perianal fistula, respectively). No recurrence of active intestinal tuberculosis occurred during follow-up in either group. The findings suggest that a 9-month treatment duration is sufficient for tuberculous enterocolitis, as it achieved comparable efficacy to the 15-month regimen in terms of complete response and absence of recurrence.
rejected:
A **9-month treatment** is generally considered **sufficient** for **tuberculous enterocolitis** in most cases, provided the patient adheres to standard **anti-tuberculosis therapy (ATT)** (e.g., 2 months of intensive phase with **rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol**, followed by 7 months of **rifampin and isoniazid**). However, treatment duration may be extended to **12 months** in cases of **severe disease, delayed response, or immunosuppression (e.g., HIV co-infection)**. Always follow local guidelines and assess clinical response.  

Would you like details on monitoring or alternative regimens?

prompt:
Interference from irrelevant negative material might be a key mechanism underlying intrusive ruminative thoughts in depression. Considering commonalities between depression and social anxiety and the presence of similar intrusive thoughts in social anxiety, the current study was designed to assess whether interference from irrelevant material in working memory is specific to depression or is also present in social anxiety disorder.
To examine the effects of irrelevant emotional material on working memory performance, participants memorized two lists of words on each trial and were subsequently instructed to ignore one of the lists. Participants were then asked to indicate whether a probe word belonged to the relevant list or not.
Compared to control and social anxiety groups, the depression groups (both pure and comorbid with social anxiety disorder) exhibited greater difficulties removing irrelevant emotional material from working memory (i.e., greater intrusion effects). Greater intrusion effects were also associated with increased rumination.
Although we included three clinical groups (depression, social anxiety, and the comorbid groups), the results are based on a relatively small number of participants.

Q: Updating emotional content in working memory: a depression-specific deficit?
A:
chosen: The study examined whether interference from irrelevant emotional material in working memory is specific to depression or also present in social anxiety disorder. Participants memorized two lists of words and were instructed to ignore one, then asked to indicate if a probe word belonged to the relevant list. Compared to control and social anxiety groups, the depression groups (both pure and comorbid with social anxiety disorder) exhibited greater difficulties removing irrelevant emotional material from working memory, showing greater intrusion effects. Greater intrusion effects were also associated with increased rumination. The results are based on a relatively small number of participants across three clinical groups (depression, social anxiety, and the comorbid groups).
rejected: The study suggests that individuals with depression may have a specific deficit in updating emotional content in working memory, particularly for negative information, compared to healthy controls. This impairment could contribute to the maintenance of negative biases seen in depression. However, more research is needed to confirm whether this deficit is unique to depression or also present in other mood disorders.

We have previously reported the feasibility of diagnostic and therapeutic peritoneoscopy including liver biopsy, gastrojejunostomy, and tubal ligation by an oral transgastric approach. We present results of per-oral transgastric splenectomy in a porcine model. The goal of this study was to determine the technical feasibility of per-oral transgastric splenectomy using a flexible endoscope.
We performed acute experiments on 50-kg pigs. All animals were fed liquids for 3 days prior to procedure. The procedures were performed under general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation. The flexible endoscope was passed per orally into the stomach and puncture of the gastric wall was performed with a needle knife. The puncture was extended to create a 1.5-cm incision using a pull-type sphincterotome, and a double-channel endoscope was advanced into the peritoneal cavity. The peritoneal cavity was insufflated with air through the endoscope. The spleen was visualized. The splenic vessels were ligated with endoscopic loops and clips, and then mesentery was dissected using electrocautery.
Endoscopic splenectomy was performed on six pigs. There were no complications during gastric incision and entrance into the peritoneal cavity. Visualization of the spleen and other intraperitoneal organs was very good. Ligation of the splenic vessels and mobilization of the spleen were achieved using commercially available devices and endoscopic accessories.

Q: Transgastric endoscopic splenectomy: is it possible?
A:
chosen:
Yes, per-oral transgastric splenectomy is possible, as shown in a porcine model using a flexible endoscope. The study performed acute experiments on 50-kg pigs fed liquids for 3 days prior to the procedure. The procedures were performed under general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation. The flexible endoscope was passed per orally into the stomach, and puncture of the gastric wall was performed with a needle knife. The puncture was extended to create a 1.5-cm incision using a pull-type sphincterotome, and a double-channel endoscope was advanced into the peritoneal cavity. The peritoneal cavity was insufflated with air through the endoscope. The spleen was visualized, and the splenic vessels were ligated with endoscopic loops and clips, followed by dissection of the mesentery using electrocautery.  

Endoscopic splenectomy was performed on six pigs with no complications during gastric incision and entrance into the peritoneal cavity. Visualization of the spleen and other intraperitoneal organs was very good, and ligation of the splenic vessels and mobilization of the spleen were achieved using commercially available devices and endoscopic accessories.
rejected:
Yes, transgastric endoscopic splenectomy is technically possible and has been performed in experimental and select clinical settings, primarily using **natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (NOTES)** techniques. However, it remains a **challenging and investigational** procedure due to risks such as bleeding, infection, and difficulty in achieving hemostasis and complete splenic extraction.  

Current clinical practice still favors **laparoscopic splenectomy** as the gold standard for minimally invasive splenic removal, but advances in endoscopic tools and techniques may expand the role of transgastric approaches in the future.

prompt:
Quality of Life (QoL) assessment remains integral in the investigation of women with lower urinary tract dysfunction. Previous work suggests that physicians tend to underestimate patients' symptoms and the bother that they cause. The aim of this study was to assess the relationship between physician and patient assessed QoL using the Kings Health Questionnaire (KHQ).
Patients complaining of troublesome lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) were recruited from a tertiary referral urodynamic clinic. Prior to their clinic appointment they were sent a KHQ, which was completed before attending. After taking a detailed urogynecological history, a second KHQ was filled in by the physician, blinded to the patient responses, on the basis of their impression of the symptoms elicited during the interview. These data were analyzed by an independent statistician. Concordance between patient and physician assessment for individual questions was assessed using weighted kappa analysis. QoL scores were compared using Wilcoxons signed rank test.
Seventy-five patients were recruited over a period of 5 months. Overall, the weighted kappa showed relatively poor concordance between the patient and physician responses; mean kappa: 0.33 (range 0.18-0.57). The physician underestimated QoL score in 4/9 domains by a mean of 5.5% and overestimated QoL score in 5/9 domains by a mean of 6.9%. In particular, physicians underestimated the impact of LUTS on social limitations and emotions (P<0.05).

Q: Is there a discrepancy between patient and physician quality of life assessment?
A:
chosen: The study assessed the relationship between physician and patient-assessed Quality of Life (QoL) using the Kings Health Questionnaire (KHQ) in women with lower urinary tract dysfunction. Previous work suggests that physicians tend to underestimate patients' symptoms and the bother they cause. The results showed relatively poor concordance between patient and physician responses, with a mean weighted kappa of 0.33 (range 0.18-0.57). Physicians underestimated QoL scores in 4 out of 9 domains by a mean of 5.5% and overestimated QoL scores in 5 out of 9 domains by a mean of 6.9%. Specifically, physicians underestimated the impact of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) on social limitations and emotions, with statistical significance (P<0.05). The findings indicate a discrepancy between patient and physician QoL assessments.
rejected: Yes, there is often a discrepancy between patient and physician assessments of quality of life (QoL). Studies show that physicians tend to underestimate or misjudge patients' QoL compared to patients' self-reports, particularly in chronic or severe illnesses. This gap may arise from differences in perspectives, communication barriers, or physicians focusing more on clinical outcomes than subjective well-being. Patient-reported outcomes (PROs) are increasingly recommended to improve accuracy in QoL assessments.

prompt:
The influence of positron emission tomography in the management of recurrent rectal cancer is well established but its role in primary rectal cancer remains uncertain. This study therefore prospectively assesses the impact of position emission tomography scanning on the management of primary rectal cancer.
Forty-six patients with advanced primary rectal cancer referred for consideration of adjuvant preoperative therapy underwent position emission tomography scanning. The referring physicians prospectively recorded each patient's stage following conventional imaging and the proposed treatment plan prior to position emission tomography scanning. This was then compared with subsequent stage and actual management implemented, and the appropriateness of position emission tomography-induced changes was noted by subsequent clinical follow-up.
The surgical management of 36 of 46 patients (78 percent) was unchanged as a result of position emission tomography, even though position emission tomography upstaged disease in 3 of 36 cases (8 percent) and downstaged disease in 5 of 36 cases (14 percent). In 8 of 46 cases (17 percent), management was altered because of the position emission tomography scan findings, including 6 cases (13 percent) in which surgery was cancelled and 2 other cases (4 percent) in which the radiotherapy field was changed. Where available, follow-up confirmed the appropriateness of position emission tomography-induced management change in each case. Two patients had a change in therapy independent of the position emission tomography scan due to clinical circumstances. Overall tumor stage was changed following position emission tomography in 18 of 46 patients (39 percent).

Q: Does positron emission tomography change management in primary rectal cancer?
A:
chosen:
Yes, positron emission tomography (PET) changed management in 17% of cases (8 of 46) in primary rectal cancer.  

In 8 of 46 cases (17 percent), management was altered because of the PET scan findings, including 6 cases (13 percent) in which surgery was cancelled and 2 other cases (4 percent) in which the radiotherapy field was changed. Follow-up confirmed the appropriateness of PET-induced management change in each case.  

The surgical management of 36 of 46 patients (78 percent) was unchanged as a result of PET, even though PET upstaged disease in 3 of 36 cases (8 percent) and downstaged disease in 5 of 36 cases (14 percent). Overall tumor stage was changed following PET in 18 of 46 patients (39 percent). Two patients had a change in therapy independent of the PET scan due to clinical circumstances.  

The role of PET in primary rectal cancer remains uncertain, though its influence in recurrent rectal cancer is well established.
rejected:
Yes, positron emission tomography (PET) can change management in primary rectal cancer, particularly in detecting distant metastases (e.g., liver, lung) that may alter staging and treatment plans (e.g., shifting from curative to palliative intent or modifying surgical/radiation approaches). However, its routine use is not universally recommended due to limited added value over conventional imaging (MRI/CT) for local staging. PET is most impactful in cases with equivocal findings or suspected recurrence.
