prompt:
To evaluate the degree to which histologic chorioamnionitis, a frequent finding in placentas submitted for histopathologic evaluation, correlates with clinical indicators of infection in the mother.
A retrospective review was performed on 52 cases with a histologic diagnosis of acute chorioamnionitis from 2,051 deliveries at University Hospital, Newark, from January 2003 to July 2003. Third-trimester placentas without histologic chorioamnionitis (n = 52) served as controls. Cases and controls were selected sequentially. Maternal medical records were reviewed for indicators of maternal infection.
Histologic chorioamnionitis was significantly associated with the usage of antibiotics (p = 0.0095) and a higher mean white blood cell count (p = 0.018). The presence of 1 or more clinical indicators was significantly associated with the presence of histologic chorioamnionitis (p = 0.019).

Q: Does histologic chorioamnionitis correspond to clinical chorioamnionitis?
A:
chosen: Histologic chorioamnionitis was significantly associated with the usage of antibiotics and a higher mean white blood cell count. The presence of 1 or more clinical indicators was significantly associated with the presence of histologic chorioamnionitis. These findings suggest a correlation between histologic chorioamnionitis and clinical indicators of infection in the mother.
rejected: No, histologic chorioamnionitis (inflammation seen on placental pathology) does not always correspond to clinical chorioamnionitis (symptoms like maternal fever, uterine tenderness, or fetal tachycardia). While there is overlap, many cases of histologic chorioamnionitis are subclinical (asymptomatic), and not all clinical cases show histologic evidence. The two terms describe related but distinct conditions.

prompt:
Dyschesia can be provoked by inappropriate defecation movements. The aim of this prospective study was to demonstrate dysfunction of the anal sphincter and/or the musculus (m.) puborectalis in patients with dyschesia using anorectal endosonography.
Twenty consecutive patients with a medical history of dyschesia and a control group of 20 healthy subjects underwent linear anorectal endosonography (Toshiba models IUV 5060 and PVL-625 RT). In both groups, the dimensions of the anal sphincter and the m. puborectalis were measured at rest, and during voluntary squeezing and straining. Statistical analysis was performed within and between the two groups.
The anal sphincter became paradoxically shorter and/or thicker during straining (versus the resting state) in 85% of patients but in only 35% of control subjects. Changes in sphincter length were statistically significantly different (p<0.01, chi(2) test) in patients compared with control subjects. The m. puborectalis became paradoxically shorter and/or thicker during straining in 80% of patients but in only 30% of controls. Both the changes in length and thickness of the m. puborectalis were significantly different (p<0.01, chi(2) test) in patients versus control subjects.

Q: Is anorectal endosonography valuable in dyschesia?
A:
chosen:
This prospective study demonstrates that anorectal endosonography is valuable for identifying dysfunction of the anal sphincter and/or the musculus puborectalis in patients with dyschesia.  

Twenty consecutive patients with dyschesia and 20 healthy controls underwent linear anorectal endosonography (Toshiba models IUV 5060 and PVL-625 RT), with measurements taken at rest, during voluntary squeezing, and during straining. Statistical analysis was performed within and between the two groups.  

The anal sphincter became paradoxically shorter and/or thicker during straining in 85% of patients compared to 35% of control subjects, with statistically significant differences in sphincter length changes (p<0.01, chi(2) test). Similarly, the m. puborectalis became paradoxically shorter and/or thicker during straining in 80% of patients versus 30% of controls, with significant differences in both length and thickness changes (p<0.01, chi(2) test).  

These findings confirm that anorectal endosonography effectively detects functional abnormalities of the anal sphincter and m. puborectalis in dyschesia patients.
rejected: Yes, anorectal endosonography is valuable in dyschesia as it helps evaluate structural abnormalities of the anal sphincters, pelvic floor, and surrounding tissues, which may contribute to defecatory dysfunction. It is particularly useful in identifying sphincter defects, rectal intussusception, or other anatomical causes of obstructed defecation. However, it is often used alongside other diagnostic tools like manometry and defecography for a comprehensive assessment.

prompt:
This study provides the first large-volume (1000 implant) comparison of the deflation rates of Poly Implant Prosthesis prefilled textured saline breast implants versus a control group of Mentor Siltex textured saline implants.
A consecutive series of 500 Poly Implant Prosthesis prefilled textured saline breast implants was compared with a consecutive series of 500 Mentor Siltex breast implants. Each breast implant was evaluated for a 4-year period, and the annual deflation rate (number of deflations during a given year divided by the total number of implants) and cumulative deflation rate (cumulative total of deflations through a given year divided by the total number of implants) were recorded. Statistical significance was calculated using the Fisher's exact test at year 1 and the chi-square analysis at years 2 through 4.
The cumulative deflation rates of the Poly Implant Prosthesis implants was as follows: year 1, 1.2 percent; year 2, 5.6 percent; year 3, 11.4 percent; and year 4, 15.4 percent. The cumulative deflation rates of the Mentor implants was: year 1, 0.2 percent; year 2, 0.6 percent; year 3, 1.6 percent; and year 4, 4.4 percent. At year 1, the difference between deflation rates was not statistically significant (Fisher's exact test, p>0.05). However, at year 2 (chi-square, 13.29; p<0.001), year 3 (chi-square, 37.91; p<0.001), and year 4 (chi-square, 32.69; p<0.001), the difference was statistically significant.

Q: A comparison of 500 prefilled textured saline breast implants versus 500 standard textured saline breast implants: is there a difference in deflation rates?
A:
chosen: The study compared 500 Poly Implant Prosthesis prefilled textured saline breast implants with 500 Mentor Siltex textured saline implants over a 4-year period, recording annual and cumulative deflation rates. The cumulative deflation rates of the Poly Implant Prosthesis implants were as follows: year 1, 1.2 percent; year 2, 5.6 percent; year 3, 11.4 percent; and year 4, 15.4 percent. The cumulative deflation rates of the Mentor implants were: year 1, 0.2 percent; year 2, 0.6 percent; year 3, 1.6 percent; and year 4, 4.4 percent. At year 1, the difference between deflation rates was not statistically significant (Fisher's exact test, p>0.05). However, at year 2 (chi-square, 13.29; p<0.001), year 3 (chi-square, 37.91; p<0.001), and year 4 (chi-square, 32.69; p<0.001), the difference was statistically significant. Poly Implant Prosthesis implants had significantly higher deflation rates after year 1.
rejected:
A comparison of 500 prefilled textured saline breast implants versus 500 standard textured saline breast implants would likely show **no significant difference in deflation rates**, as the primary factors influencing deflation (e.g., shell integrity, valve mechanism, surgical technique, and patient factors) are generally similar between prefilled and standard saline implants.  

However, if the prefilled implants have a more robust valve or manufacturing quality control, deflation rates *might* be slightly lower. Conversely, if standard implants are filled intraoperatively with more precise volume control, they *might* experience marginally fewer deflations due to reduced overfilling stress.  

Current evidence does not strongly suggest a major difference in deflation rates between the two types, but a direct study would be needed for definitive conclusions.

prompt:
A growing body of research emphasizes the importance of contextual factors on health outcomes. Using postcode sector data for Scotland (UK), this study tests the hypothesis of spatial heterogeneity in the relationship between area-level deprivation and mortality to determine if contextual differences in the West vs. the rest of Scotland influence this relationship. Research into health inequalities frequently fails to recognise spatial heterogeneity in the deprivation-health relationship, assuming that global relationships apply uniformly across geographical areas. In this study, exploratory spatial data analysis methods are used to assess local patterns in deprivation and mortality. Spatial regression models are then implemented to examine the relationship between deprivation and mortality more formally.
The initial exploratory spatial data analysis reveals concentrations of high standardized mortality ratios (SMR) and deprivation (hotspots) in the West of Scotland and concentrations of low values (coldspots) for both variables in the rest of the country. The main spatial regression result is that deprivation is the only variable that is highly significantly correlated with all-cause mortality in all models. However, in contrast to the expected spatial heterogeneity in the deprivation-mortality relationship, this relation does not vary between regions in any of the models. This result is robust to a number of specifications, including weighting for population size, controlling for spatial autocorrelation and heteroskedasticity, assuming a non-linear relationship between mortality and socio-economic deprivation, separating the dependent variable into male and female SMRs, and distinguishing between West, North and Southeast regions. The rejection of the hypothesis of spatial heterogeneity in the relationship between socio-economic deprivation and mortality complements prior research on the stability of the deprivation-mortality relationship over time.

Q: Does context matter for the relationship between deprivation and all-cause mortality?
A:
chosen: A growing body of research emphasizes the importance of contextual factors on health outcomes, but this study found that the relationship between deprivation and all-cause mortality does not vary between regions. The exploratory spatial data analysis revealed concentrations of high standardized mortality ratios and deprivation in the West of Scotland and low values in the rest of the country. However, the spatial regression results showed that deprivation is the only variable highly significantly correlated with all-cause mortality in all models, and this relationship does not vary between regions. The rejection of spatial heterogeneity in the deprivation-mortality relationship is robust to multiple specifications, including population size weighting, controlling for spatial autocorrelation and heteroskedasticity, and separating the dependent variable into male and female SMRs. This finding complements prior research on the stability of the deprivation-mortality relationship over time.
rejected: Yes, context significantly matters for the relationship between deprivation and all-cause mortality. Factors such as healthcare access, social policies, economic conditions, and environmental stressors can modify the strength and nature of this relationship across different regions and populations. For example, stronger welfare systems may mitigate the impact of deprivation on mortality, while areas with poor infrastructure may see a stronger association.

To determine the effect of prior benign prostate biopsies on the surgical and clinical outcomes of patients treated with radical perineal prostatectomy for prostate cancer.
A total of 1369 patients with clinically localized prostate cancer underwent radical prostatectomy by a single surgeon between 1991 and 2001. A subset of 203 patients (14.9%), who had undergone at least one prior benign prostate biopsy for a rising prostate-specific antigen and/or abnormal digital rectal examination, constituted our study population. A total of 1115 patients with no prior biopsy represented our control group. After prostatectomy, patients were evaluated at 6-month intervals for biochemical evidence of recurrence, defined as a prostate-specific antigen level of 0.5 ng/mL or greater.
Patients with a prior benign biopsy had more favorable pathologic features with more organ-confined (74% versus 64%; P<0.001) and less margin-positive (9.8% versus 18%) disease. Only 24 patients (12%) in the study group (versus 20% in control group; P = 0.01) had eventual evidence of biochemical failure. Kaplan-Meier analyses suggested that patients with prior benign biopsies have improved biochemical disease-free survival, especially for those with more aggressive disease (Gleason sum 7 or greater; P<0.01). Overall, patients in the study group had lower probability (odds ratio 0.57, P<0.001) of biochemical failure compared with those in the control group.

Q: Does prior benign prostate biopsy predict outcome for patients treated with radical perineal prostatectomy?
A:
chosen: Patients with a prior benign prostate biopsy had more favorable pathologic features, including more organ-confined disease (74% versus 64%; P<0.001) and less margin-positive disease (9.8% versus 18%). Only 12% of the study group (versus 20% in the control group; P = 0.01) had eventual evidence of biochemical failure, defined as a prostate-specific antigen level of 0.5 ng/mL or greater. Kaplan-Meier analyses showed improved biochemical disease-free survival for patients with prior benign biopsies, especially for those with more aggressive disease (Gleason sum 7 or greater; P<0.01). Overall, the study group had a lower probability of biochemical failure (odds ratio 0.57, P<0.001) compared with the control group.
rejected:
Prior benign prostate biopsy does not appear to significantly predict adverse outcomes (e.g., biochemical recurrence, positive surgical margins, or pathological upgrading) in patients undergoing radical perineal prostatectomy (RPP). Studies suggest that RPP remains effective regardless of prior biopsy findings, with comparable oncologic and functional outcomes to those without a history of benign biopsy. However, individual risk factors (e.g., PSA levels, clinical stage) should still guide management.  

For a definitive answer, consult recent studies or meta-analyses specific to RPP and prior benign biopsies.

prompt:
The temporal pattern of the biologic mechanism linking red blood cell (RBC) storage duration with clinical outcomes is yet unknown. This study investigates how such a temporal pattern can affect the power of randomized controlled trials (RCT) to detect a relevant clinical outcome mediated by the transfusion of stored RBCs.
This study was a computer simulation of four RCTs, each using a specific categorization of the RBC storage time. The trial's endpoint was evaluated assuming five hypothetical temporal patterns for the biologic mechanism linking RBC storage duration with clinical outcomes.
Power of RCTs to unveil a significant association between RBC storage duration and clinical outcomes was critically dependent on a complex interaction among three factors: 1) the way the RBC storage time is categorized in the trial design, 2) the temporal pattern assumed for the RBC storage lesion, and 3) the age distribution of RBCs in the inventory from which they are picked up for transfusion. For most combinations of these factors, the power of RCTs to detect a significant treatment effect was below 80%. All the four simulated RCTs had a very low power to disclose a harmful clinical effect confined to last week of the maximum 42-day shelf life of stored RBCs.

Q: Will clinical studies elucidate the connection between the length of storage of transfused red blood cells and clinical outcomes?
A:
chosen: The temporal pattern of the biologic mechanism linking red blood cell (RBC) storage duration with clinical outcomes is yet unknown. This study, a computer simulation of four randomized controlled trials (RCTs), investigated how such a temporal pattern can affect the power of RCTs to detect a relevant clinical outcome mediated by the transfusion of stored RBCs. The power of RCTs to unveil a significant association between RBC storage duration and clinical outcomes was critically dependent on a complex interaction among three factors: 1) the way the RBC storage time is categorized in the trial design, 2) the temporal pattern assumed for the RBC storage lesion, and 3) the age distribution of RBCs in the inventory from which they are picked up for transfusion. For most combinations of these factors, the power of RCTs to detect a significant treatment effect was below 80%. All four simulated RCTs had a very low power to disclose a harmful clinical effect confined to the last week of the maximum 42-day shelf life of stored RBCs.
rejected: The connection between the length of storage of transfused red blood cells (RBCs) and clinical outcomes remains uncertain despite multiple clinical studies. Some trials suggest potential risks with older RBCs (e.g., increased complications), while others show no significant difference. Ongoing and future studies, including large randomized controlled trials (RCTs), may provide more definitive answers, but current evidence is inconclusive.

Elevated resting heart rate (RHR) is a neglected marker in cardiovascular risk factor studies of sub-Saharan African populations. This study aimed to determine the prevalence of elevated RHR and other risk factors for cardiovascular disease (CVD) and to investigate any associations between RHR and these risk factors in a rural population in Ghana.
Cross-sectional analysis.
A total of 574 adults aged between 18-65 years were randomly sampled from a population register. Data collected included those on sociodemographic variables and anthropometric, blood pressure (BP), and RHR measurements. Within-person variability in RHR was calculated using data from repeat measurements taken 2 weeks apart.
Of study participants, 36% were male. Prevalence of casual high BP was 19%. In the population, 10% were current cigarette smokers and habitual alcohol use was high at 56%. As measured by body mass index, 2% were obese and 14% had abdominal obesity. RHR was elevated (>90 bpm) in 19%. Overall, 79% of study participants were found to have at least one CVD risk factor. RHR was significantly associated with age, waist circumference, and BP. Individuals with an elevated RHR had a higher risk (OR 1.94, 95% CI 1.15-3.26%, p = 0.013) of casual high BP compared with participants with normal RHR independently of several established CVD risk factors. The regression dilution ratio of RHR was 0.75 (95% CI 0.62-0.89).

Q: Cardiovascular risk in a rural adult West African population: is resting heart rate also relevant?
A:
chosen: Elevated resting heart rate (RHR) is a neglected marker in cardiovascular risk factor studies of sub-Saharan African populations. In a rural Ghanaian population of 574 adults aged 18-65 years, 19% had an elevated RHR (>90 bpm). The prevalence of casual high blood pressure (BP) was 19%, while 10% were current cigarette smokers and 56% reported habitual alcohol use. Obesity prevalence was 2% by body mass index and 14% had abdominal obesity. Overall, 79% of participants had at least one cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk factor. RHR was significantly associated with age, waist circumference, and BP. Individuals with elevated RHR had a higher risk (OR 1.94, 95% CI 1.15-3.26, p = 0.013) of casual high BP compared to those with normal RHR, independent of other established CVD risk factors. The regression dilution ratio of RHR was 0.75 (95% CI 0.62-0.89).
rejected: Yes, resting heart rate (RHR) is relevant as a cardiovascular risk factor in rural adult West African populations. Studies suggest that elevated RHR is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, similar to findings in other populations, though regional variations in risk factors and healthcare access may influence its impact. Further research is needed to refine risk stratification in this specific demographic.

prompt:
Opioid-dependent patients often have co-occurring chronic illnesses requiring medications that interact with methadone. Methadone maintenance treatment (MMT) is typically provided separately from medical care. Hence, coordination of medical care and substance use treatment is important to preserve patient safety.
To identify potential safety risks among MMT patients engaged in medical care by evaluating the frequency that opioid dependence and MMT documentation are missing in medical records and characterizing potential medication-methadone interactions.
Among patients from a methadone clinic who received primary care from an affiliated, but separate, medical center, we reviewed electronic medical records for documentation of methadone, opioid dependence, and potential drug-methadone interactions. The proportions of medical records without opioid dependence and methadone documentation were estimated and potential medication-methadone interactions were identified.
Among the study subjects (n = 84), opioid dependence documentation was missing from the medical record in 30% (95% CI, 20%-41%) and MMT documentation was missing from either the last primary care note or the last hospital discharge summary in 11% (95% CI, 5%-19%). Sixty-nine percent of the study subjects had at least 1 medication that potentially interacted with methadone; 19% had 3 or more potentially interacting medications.

Q: Are opioid dependence and methadone maintenance treatment (MMT) documented in the medical record?
A:
chosen: Opioid dependence documentation was missing from the medical record in 30% (95% CI, 20%-41%) of the study subjects, and MMT documentation was missing from either the last primary care note or the last hospital discharge summary in 11% (95% CI, 5%-19%). The study reviewed electronic medical records for documentation of methadone, opioid dependence, and potential drug-methadone interactions among patients from a methadone clinic who received primary care from an affiliated medical center. Sixty-nine percent of the study subjects had at least 1 medication that potentially interacted with methadone.
rejected: Yes, opioid dependence and methadone maintenance treatment (MMT) are typically documented in the medical record. Opioid dependence is recorded as a diagnosis, while MMT is documented as part of the treatment plan, including dosing, duration, and monitoring. Proper documentation ensures continuity of care and regulatory compliance.

prompt:
The purpose of this study is to determine if baseline antral follicle assessment may serve as additional information in predicting in vitro fertilization outcome.
Prospective, descriptive preliminary study of in vitro fertilization outcome. From July 1998 to July 1999, 224 patients underwent antral follicle assessment (follicle 2-6 mm in diameter) on baseline of the planned, stimulated in vitro fertilization cycle. The outcomes were analyzed with respect to antral follicle assessment (<or = 6 or>6), basal cycle day 3 follicle stimulated hormone (<or = 10 or>10 IU/L) and maternal age (<or = 35 or>35 years).
The clinical pregnancy rate was significantly higher in the group with baseline antral follicle>6 compared to that in the group with antral follicle<or = 6 (51% vs. 19%, respectively). Controlling for patient age, and basal follicle stimulated hormone, the pregnancy rate was significantly higher in the group with antral follicle>6 compared to that in the group with antral follicle<or = 6. The cancellation rate was significantly increased with advancing maternal age, elevated basal follicle stimulated hormone levels, and baseline antral follicle<or = 6. The cancellation rate was significantly higher in the group with antral follicle<or = 6 compared to that in the group with antral follicle>or = 6 (33% vs. 1%, respectively).

Q: Antral follicle assessment as a tool for predicting outcome in IVF--is it a better predictor than age and FSH?
A:
chosen: The study aimed to determine if baseline antral follicle assessment could serve as additional information in predicting in vitro fertilization outcome. The clinical pregnancy rate was significantly higher in the group with baseline antral follicle count >6 compared to the group with ≤6 (51% vs. 19%). Controlling for age and basal follicle-stimulating hormone, the pregnancy rate remained significantly higher in the group with antral follicle count >6. The cancellation rate was significantly higher in the group with antral follicle count ≤6 compared to >6 (33% vs. 1%). The cancellation rate also increased with advancing maternal age and elevated basal follicle-stimulating hormone levels. The results suggest antral follicle assessment provides predictive value beyond age and FSH levels.
rejected: Antral follicle count (AFC) is a strong predictor of ovarian reserve and IVF outcomes, often outperforming age and basal FSH alone. While age remains a key determinant of egg quality, AFC provides a more direct measure of ovarian response. Combined assessment (AFC + age ± AMH) typically offers the best predictive value for IVF success. However, AFC alone may not fully account for qualitative aspects of ovarian aging.

prompt:
We retrospectively identified 84 consecutive patients aged ≥80 years, who underwent a cardiac reoperation at the department for Cardiothoracic Surgery in the Heart&Vessel Center Bad Bevensen between January 2007 and 2013. Demographic profiles as well as operative data were analyzed, and the patients were prospectively followed. Patient's functional status and quality of life were assessed with the Barthel Index, New York Heart Association class and the short form-12 questionnaire.
The mean age of the study group (61 men, 23 women) was 81.9 ± 1.9 years. Most redo-procedures were carried out after primary coronary artery bypass grafting (65%), primary aortic valve replacement (21%) and primary mitral valve replacement (6%). The most frequent actual surgical procedures were combined coronary artery bypass grafting and aortic valve replacement (26%), isolated coronary artery bypass grafting (19%), and isolated aortic valve replacement (19%). The mean length of hospital stay was 17 ± 15 days. In-hospital mortality counted for 32.1%. During follow up (29 ± 20 months) a further 19.0% of the patients died. The Barthel Index of the survivors was 89 ± 17 and their mean New York Heart Association class was 2 ± 1. A total of 93% of the patients were living at home. Summary scores of physical and mental health of the short form-12 questionnaire equalled those of an age- and sex-matched normative population.

Q: Cardiac reoperations in octogenarians: Do they really benefit?
A:
chosen: The study retrospectively analyzed 84 consecutive patients aged ≥80 years who underwent cardiac reoperations at the Heart&Vessel Center Bad Bevensen between 2007 and 2013. The mean age was 81.9 ± 1.9 years, with 61 men and 23 women. Most redo-procedures followed primary coronary artery bypass grafting (65%), primary aortic valve replacement (21%), and primary mitral valve replacement (6%). The most frequent actual procedures were combined coronary artery bypass grafting and aortic valve replacement (26%), isolated coronary artery bypass grafting (19%), and isolated aortic valve replacement (19%). The mean hospital stay was 17 ± 15 days, with an in-hospital mortality of 32.1%. During follow-up (29 ± 20 months), an additional 19.0% of patients died. Survivors had a Barthel Index of 89 ± 17 and a mean New York Heart Association class of 2 ± 1, with 93% living at home. The short form-12 questionnaire scores for physical and mental health matched those of an age- and sex-matched normative population.
rejected:
Cardiac reoperations in carefully selected octogenarians can provide survival and quality-of-life benefits, but the risks are higher due to age-related comorbidities and frailty. Outcomes depend on patient selection, surgical complexity, and preoperative functional status. While some studies show acceptable short- and mid-term survival, the overall benefit must be weighed against conservative management. Shared decision-making is essential.
